This work is novel in integrating MobileNetV2 with Transformer encoder blocks, which enables the capture of local and global contextual features within conjunctival images by the model. Different from the current approaches that are purely CNN-based, this hybrid architecture maintains lightweight efficiency while improving diagnostic precision. The ROC-AUC was 0.96 and the accuracy was 89%. It was necessary to ensure the model focuses on medically relevant regions of conjunctiva by using LIME for visual interpretability to build clinical trust. The proposed system offers an accurate, easily accessible scalable result for early anemia screening in resource-poor settings.

title: LIARS: Low-Information Area Recognition for Steganography
authors: Claudia Larramendi-Ferras, Alexander Perez-Pons, Gustavo Chaparro-Baquero
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Steganography conceals information within digital media, making its detection a crucial challenge in modern digital forensics. This paper introduces a framework that integrates machine learning to automatically identify near uniform regions in images, to then embed data in them, and to validate the presence of such concealed content through a controlled forensic pipeline. The proposed workflow segments images into patches, classifies them as high-information or low-information using a convolutional neural network, and embeds a textual payload exclusively within low-entropy zones. A validation process based on difference-map and optical character recognition module are used to extract and confirm the hidden text. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed work can reliably detect and reconstruct hidden payloads even when pixel intensity modifications are as subtle as ±5 RGB units, imperceptible to the human eye. The results highlight the potential of low-information modeling and adaptive AI segmentation as effective tools for explainable and forensic-oriented steganalysis.

title: Autonomous Trespassing Detection of a Surveillance Robot
authors: Yugang Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Robotics and Artificial Intelligence (ICRAI)
abstract: Surveillance robots have extensive application potential in parks, gardens, plazas, malls, commercial centers, and many industrial sites, which have been relying on security officers for patrolling on foot. In order to succeed in these important applications, a surveillance robot needs to be able to move along predefined patrolling route autonomously and detect potential illegal trespassing or anomalies without continuous human intervention. This paper focuses on autonomous surveillance using low-cost cameras. A Raspberry Pi camera module was integrated with a modified commercial mobile robot TurtleBot III, which was used as the surveillance robot platform during the experiments. Furthermore, a modified YOLO (you only look once) algorithm was implemented for autonomous trespassing detection. Extensive experiments were conducted, and the experimental results confirmed that the surveillance robot could detect a person when she/he is within 15 meters even if a portion of her/his body was not in the field of view. It was also identified that the detection performance was affected by lighting conditions and complexity of the background. Furthermore, tuning the threshold of confidence score from 50% to 45% can effectively avoid missing detection during the experiments. The experimental results will definitely benefit the development of autonomous surveillance robots.

title: A Real-Time Object Detection Model for Indoor Mobile Robots Based on YOLOv8-LCFD
authors: Qingwu Fan, Jiajing Liu
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: Efficient and robust object detection is critical for indoor mobile robots, yet dense objects, occlusions, and scale variations pose challenges in complex environments. We propose a YOLOv8-LCFD-based model for real-time object detection. Three key enhancements are introduced: (1) a Lightweight Cross-Scale Fusion Module (L-CSFM) for semantic enhancement and efficient feature fusion; (2) a dynamic detection head (DyHead) employing scale, spatial, and channel attention to handle multi-scale objects and occlusions; and (3) a Unified Intersection over Union (UIoU) loss to improve bounding-box regression in dense scenes. Experiments on the THUD and indoor_COCO datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior accuracy and robustness while maintaining real-time performance, providing a lightweight and effective solution for indoor mobile robot perception.

title: Performance Evaluation of Classical Machine Learning Models for Crime Prediction
authors: Said Salloum, Khalaf Tahat, Ahmed Mansoori, Chaker Mhamdi, Dina Tahat
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Social Networks Analysis, Management and Security (SNAMS)
abstract: Crime prediction models are intended to improve public safety and resource allocation of the police. This is an important form of research, distinct from the conventional analysis of crimes using basic statistical analysis, which may not model complex patterns of crimes. In this paper, we evaluate six traditional machine learning (ML) models, namely, Logistic Regression (LR), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Naïve Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Decision Tree (DT) on their effectiveness for crime type classification and identifying key predictors. Key performance metrics, namely, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC were used to evaluate the models. Results indicate that Logistic Regression, SVM, Naïve Bayes and Decision Tree achieved high classification performance, whereas Random Forest and KNN revealed close-to-optimal performance. The robustness of these models is further confirmed by the ROC curve analysis. These findings highlight the capabilities of traditional machine learning for predictive policing and crime prevention. For further study, we anticipate applying real-time data and deep learning techniques to build a more adaptive crime prediction model.

title: An End-Edge-Cloud Collaborative Scheduling Method Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning and Attention Mechanism Integration
authors: Xin Li, Di Fang, Miao Yu, Sai Luo, Hongchen Liu, Baichuan Lin
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: With the rapid development of the Internet of Things (IoT) and edge computing, the end-edge-cloud collaborative architecture has become the core infrastructure supporting key scenarios such as intelligent manufacturing and smart cities. However, traditional scheduling methods have significant limitations when dealing with heterogeneous resources, dynamic task requirements, and multi-objective optimization problems in end-edge-cloud environments. To this end, this paper proposes an end-edge-cloud collaborative optimization scheduling method based on the integration of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and attention mechanisms. By constructing a dynamic cognitive perception meta-model, a multi-agent game-theoretic decision-making mechanism, and an adaptive attention weight distribution mechanism, it achieves efficient resource scheduling and task collaborative orchestration in complex end-edge-cloud environments. The experimental results show that, compared with the traditional methods, this method reduces the task processing delay, improves the resource utilization rate and increases the system throughput. This research provides an innovative solution to the scheduling optimization problem in the end-edge-cloud collaborative environment and lays an important foundation for the efficient operation of emerging intelligent applications.

title: CytoDiff: AI-Driven Cytomorphology Image Synthesis for Medical Diagnostics
authors: Jan Carreras Boada, Rao Muhammad Umer, Carsten Marr
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Biomedical datasets are often constrained by stringent privacy requirements and frequently suffer from severe class imbalance. These two aspects hinder the development of accurate machine learning models. While generative AI offers a promising solution, producing synthetic images of sufficient quality for training robust classifiers remains challenging. This work addresses the classification of individual white blood cells, a critical task in diagnosing hematological malignancies such as acute myeloid leukemia (AML). We introduce CytoDiff, a stable diffusion model fine-tuned with LoRA weights and guided by few-shot samples that generates high-fidelity synthetic white blood cell images. Our approach demonstrates substantial improvements in classifier performance when training data is limited. Using a small, highly imbalanced real dataset, the addition of 5,000 synthetic images per class improved ResNet classifier accuracy from 27% to 78% (+51%). Similarly, CLIP-based classification accuracy increased from 62% to 77% (+ 15%). These results establish synthetic image generation as a valuable tool for biomedical machine learning, enhancing data coverage and facilitating secure data sharing while preserving patient privacy. Paper code is publicly available at https://github.com/JanCarreras24/CytoDiff.

title: DeepNetDetect: A Deep Learning-Based Approach for Early Anomaly Detection in Network Traffic
authors: Henry P Cyril
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Anomaly detection in network traffic has become a critical research focus within cybersecurity. Based on unsupervised learning, this paper constructs a Model for Detecting Irregularities in Network Data to solve the problems of high dimensions of abnormal traffic. This paper provides a fully connected DNN of multi-class intrusion detection. The CICIDS2017 dataset underwent thorough preprocessing, such as feature selection, and SMOTE was used, with the minority attack classes being balanced. DNN model was proposed, which included four dense layers with ReLU activations and a single sigmoid output layer, and was trained on 75: 25 data split. The experimental results are outstanding with an accuracy of 99.69, excellent recall, 99.58 precision and 99.40 F1-score. DNN model performs better than both the traditional machine learning models used, Decision Trees 80.84% accuracy, Naive Bayes (97.48% accuracy), and Auto Encoder + Logistic Regression (98.06% accuracy) and DL models such as CNN (96.50% accuracy) and LSTM (98.20% accuracy). The model is highly generalized, converges quicker, and has few false positives, which depict its suitability in practical conditions. The present research offers a strong, extensible and high accuracy framework to the contemporary network security system.

title: HOSt3R: Keypoint-Free Hand-Object 3D Reconstruction from RGB Images
authors: Anilkumar Swamy, Vincent Leroy, Philippe Weinzaepfel, Jean-Sébastien Franco, Grégory Rogez
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Hand-object 3D reconstruction has become increasingly important for applications in human-robot interaction and immersive AR/VR experiences. A common approach for object-agnostic hand-object reconstruction from RGB sequences involves a two-stage pipeline: hand-object 3D tracking followed by multi-view 3D reconstruction. However, existing methods rely on keypoint detection techniques, such as Structure from Motion (SfM) and hand-keypoint optimization, which struggle with diverse object geometries, weak textures, and mutual hand-object occlusions, limiting scalability and generalization. As a key enabler to generic and seamless, non-intrusive applicability, we propose in this work a robust, keypoint detector-free approach to estimating hand-object 3D transformations from monocular motion video/images. We further integrate this with a multi-view reconstruction pipeline to accurately recover hand-object 3D shape. Our method, named HOSt3R, is unconstrained, does not rely on pre-scanned object templates or camera intrinsics, and reaches state-of-the-art performance for the tasks of object-agnostic hand-object 3D transformation and shape estimation on the SHOWMe benchmark. We also experiment on sequences from the HO3D dataset, demonstrating generalization to unseen object categories.

title: A Lightweight Deep Learning Based Classification Models for Non-Human Identity Threat Detection
authors: Shiva Kumara
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: The rise of non-human entities such as bots and automated scripts that interact with online platforms is a significant challenge to cybersecurity. This research offers a lightweight and efficient based system to identify the source of non-human identity threats from a publicly available Initially, Twitter Human-Bot’s dataset underwent a detailed preprocessing pipeline after which feature inconsistencies were addressed and class imbalance was reduced by handling missing values, SMOTE-based class balance, label encoding, data scaling, reshaping, and normalization. Training and testing sets were then created from the processed data. The hybrid DL models, CNN-BiLSTM and GRU, advanced to capture the temporal behavior and extract the relevant features efficiently. The findings in the table show that CNN-BiLSTM and GRU models achieved the highest accuracy of 81.22% and 80.94% respectively, compared to the traditional methods, such as Adaboost, accuracy is 73.5%, DNN accuracy is 70%, CAE accuracy is 74.69%, and Logistic Regression accuracy is 77.64%. The high accuracy and reliability of the proposed method in detecting non-human objects is a development in cybersecurity because it offers a credible detection system that can effectively address identity-based threats in dynamic cyber environments.

title: Measurement Verification of the Equivalent Method for Shielding Effectiveness Based on Cylindrical Shields
authors: Jin Zusheng, Li Jianxuan, Shi Jialin, Zhang Yong, Wang Ting
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: An equivalent method has been proposed to measure the shielding effectiveness of micro and small shielding enclosures. In this paper, a set of cylindrical shields was designed and fabricated for the measurement verification of the equivalent method. An obviously accordance of the results is acquired. The results show that, 81% of the shielding effectiveness difference are less than 6dB. Considering factors such as model manufacturing errors and testing errors, it is demonstrated that the equivalent method is correct and feasible, providing an alternative means for measuring the shielding effectiveness of micro and small shielding enclosures.

title: Ship Classification of Underwater Acoustic Target Using a CNN-Transformer Approach
authors: Yi Meng, Yihong Lai
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: Underwater acoustic target classification is an important technology for achieving maritime traffic detection, ocean engineering monitoring, and marine biology classification. This study designs an underwater acoustic recognition experiment that verifies the 1D CNN transformer network architecture can directly identify the name of a specific ship without converting the measured raw signals into a 2D time-frequency plot. The experimental results verified that the accuracy of the proposed system reached 90%, and F1 reached 94.1%.

title: Valeo Near–Field: A Novel Dataset for Pedestrian Intent Detection
authors: Antonyo Musabini, Rachid Benmokhtar, Xavier Perrotton, Jagdish Bhanushali, Victor Galizzi, Bertrand Luvison
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: This paper presents a novel dataset aimed at detecting pedestrians' intentions as they approach an ego-vehicle. The dataset comprises synchronized multi-modal data, including fisheye camera feeds, lidar laser scans, ultrasonic sensor readings, and motion capture-based 3D body poses, collected across diverse real-world scenarios. Key contributions include detailed annotations of 3D body joint positions synchronized with fisheye camera images, as well as accurate 3D pedestrian positions extracted from lidar data, facilitating robust benchmarking for perception algorithms. We release a portion of the dataset along with a comprehensive benchmark suite, featuring evaluation metrics for accuracy, efficiency, and scalability on embedded systems. By addressing real-world challenges such as sensor occlusions, dynamic environments, and hardware constraints, this dataset offers a unique resource for developing and evaluating state-of-the-art algorithms in pedestrian detection, 3D pose estimation and 4D trajectory and intention prediction. Additionally, we provide baseline performance metrics using custom neural network architectures and suggest future research directions to encourage the adoption and enhancement of the dataset. This work aims to serve as a foundation for researchers seeking to advance the capabilities of intelligent vehicles in near-field scenarios. The public test-set is available on https://github.com/antonyomusabini/Valeo_NearField

title: UltraNBA Neural Bundle Adjustment for Pose Refinement in 3D Freehand Ultrasound
authors: V. Bugra Yesilkaynak, Vanessa Gonzalez Duque, Magdalena Wysocki, Mohammad Farid Azampour, Nassir Navab, Diana Mateus
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Imprecise tracking presents a major challenge in the reconstruction of 3D freehand ultrasound volumes, as even small errors can lead to significant misalignment. Calibration inaccuracies and reliance on noisy sensors further exacerbate this issue. State-of-the-art approaches typically align pixel intensities across overlapping frames. However, misalignment in ultrasound sweeps, which depend on the sensor's position, often result in inconsistent intensities for the same spatial location, challenging the reliability of these methods. To address these challenges, we propose UltraNBA, a novel implicitly neural bundle-adjusting framework for ultrasound. By leveraging the spatial consistency of acoustic tissue properties instead of plain intensity alignment, UltraNBA corrects tracking errors while capturing stable anatomical and physical representations, yielding higherquality reconstructions. Our method supports single and multiple sweeps, offering versatility in real-world clinical scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate a reduction in tracking errors, accompanied by enhanced image quality for rendering new frames. We make code and data opensource at https://github.comIMrGranddyIUltraNBA.

title: TabNet-IDS: A TabNet-Driven Tabular Deep Learning Framework for Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Md Habibur Rahman, Md Wahidur Rahman, Avdesh Mishra, Tarek Mahmud, Mais Nijim
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: The rapid growth of networked systems has increased exposure to sophisticated cyberattacks, demanding intrusion detection methods that can learn directly from complex traffic data. This paper proposes a TabNet-driven intrusion detection framework built on the UNSW-NB15 benchmark dataset. The approach first performs data cleaning, label encoding of categorical attributes, and standardization of numerical features, followed by a binary labeling scheme that groups all attack types into a single intrusion class against normal traffic. The TabNet classifier is trained using 75% of the labeled data, while 25% is reserved for validation, and its generalization is assessed on an independent unseen test set. Experimental results show strong and balanced performance, achieving a precision of 92.68%, recall of 94.23%, F1-score of 93.45%, accuracy of 92.72%, and a Matthews Correlation Coefficient of 85.28%. The ROC and Precision–Recall curves further confirm high separability between normal and intrusive flows, with areas under the curve of 0.975 and 0.977, respectively. A comparison with several representative state-of-the-art models, including GRU-based, decision tree, ensemble, and geometric-analysis methods, demonstrates that the proposed TabNet framework provides the most favorable combination of evaluation metrics, indicating its suitability for accurate and reliable network intrusion detection.

title: Research on Special Tests of VSC-HVDC Converter Valve
authors: Rong Hu, Zhekan Su, Rubin Tang, Boya Zhao, Jian Xie, Zhixiang Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Energy Power and Electrical Technology (CEPET)
abstract: Currently, the type tests of Voltage Sourced Converter (VSC) valves for High-Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) power transmission are mostly based on IEC 62501. However, this standard has certain limitations, such as its inability to assess the dielectric endurance of converter valves under coolant leakage conditions, the protective performance of IGBTs during destructive short-circuits, the bypass functionality during submodule failure, and the losses evaluation test. Drawing on the VSC converter valves design verification tests, this paper proposes additional test procedures besides the existing IEC 62501, including the most stringent dielectric test between valve terminals, wet test between valve terminals under coolant leakage condition, bypass function test in combination with IGBT destructive shortcircuit test, and losses measurement. It outlines testing methodologies and validates them through design verification test of converter valves, providing essential references for future VSCHVDC converter valve testing and updates to the IEC 62501 standard.

title: Real-Time Embedded Vision System for Plastic Bag Detection on Raspberry Pi 5
authors: Rishi Jayanath A, Adwaith Sajikumar, Johann Shoni George, G. Jaspher W. Kathrine
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: Plastic bags have become one of the most troublesome forms of waste because they are cheap, used everywhere, and do not break down easily. Once thrown away, they often end up blocking drains, floating in rivers, or scattered in natural spaces, causing serious problems for both the environment and infrastructure. Sorting this waste by hand is slow and unreliable, while large automated systems are usually too expensive for small communities or field applications. This work presents a simple and affordable system that can detect plastic bags using a Raspberry Pi 5 and a lightweight object detection model. The model was trained with a custom dataset of 2,909 labeled images through Roboflow and deployed using YOLOv11. The device captures video at a modest resolution of 320×240 pixels and 10 frames per second. Each frame is sent through a cloud-based API for analysis, and the results are improved with extra image filtering to cut down on false alarms. Whenever a bag is detected, the system sends a push notification with the image and details to the user. Tests showed strong results, with the model reaching 99.0% mAP, 99.4% precision, and 97.8% recall. Overall, the design is practical, low-cost, and flexible enough for real-world waste monitoring.

title: Enhanced Fisheye Object Detection via YOLO Ensemble Learning and Weighted Box Fusion
authors: Chun-Ming Tsai, Li-Li Wu, Tsung-Yu Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Fisheye cameras offer ultra-wide fields of view for urban surveillance and autonomous systems, but introduce severe radial distortion, making object detection significantly more challenging than with perspective imagery. In this paper, we propose a calibration-free, ensemble-based object detection framework tailored for distorted fisheye images. Our approach combines three complementary YOLO-based detectors-SPPCSPC-YOLOR-D6, YOLOv10X, and YOLOv13L-each trainedon domain-rich datasets including FishEye8K, VisDrone, and pseudo-labeled data. To improve robustness under challenging lighting conditions, we apply gamma correction during inference. Final predictions are merged using Weighted Box Fusion (WBF), which consolidates overlapping detections based on spatial and confidence agreement. Evaluated on the FishEye1Keval dataset from the AI City Challenge 2025, our method achieves an F1-score of 0.6413, ranking second among 62 competing teams. These results highlight the effectiveness of ensemble learning, domain adaptation, and post-inference fusion in addressing fisheye-specific challenges in object detection. The code for our models is available at: https://github.com/yvnneno003/UT_2025_T4.

title: A Cascaded Metasurface Antenna Topology for Measurement-Efficient Phaseless Computational Microwave Imaging via Diversity Multiplication
authors: Yutang Dong, Wei Kang, Jingyi Mao, Minquan Wei, Chenwei Zhou, Boyu Sima, Wen Wu
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: Phaseless computational microwave imaging (PCMI) simplifies system hardware but requires high measurement diversity for accurate reconstruction. This work proposes a cascaded metasurface antenna topology to enhance measurement efficiency by multiplying diversity. The system cascades a frequency-diverse metasurface antenna (FDMA) with a space-time-modulated programmable metasurface (STPM). Through simulation, we demonstrate that this approach jointly fuses frequency and harmonic diversity, generating multiple unique radiation patterns from a single physical measurement. The simulation results, validated by a significant reduction in mean squared error (MSE), confirm that this diversity multiplication principle effectively improves reconstruction quality without needing additional bandwidth or measurements, paving the way for more efficient PCMI systems.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning-Based Configuration of Mobile Network Antenna Parameters
authors: Saidiwaerdi Maimaiti, Xuewen Liu, Dongyan Zhang
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: With the rapid development of mobile communications, cellular networks have become a crucial infrastructure supporting people's daily lives and work. As the number of users continues to grow, maintaining network performance in dense areas has become increasingly challenging. Among various base station parameters, the antenna down-tilt plays a critical role in determining system throughput and stability. However, existing optimization methods often fail to explicitly focus on these two metrics. In this study, we propose and implement three antenna down-tilt optimization schemes based on reinforcement learning, including the Q-learning algorithm and two multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches: Value-Decomposition Networks (VDN) and Q-value Mixing (QMIX). Furthermore, a dynamic adjustment mechanism for reinforcement learning parameters is designed according to throughput performance, aiming to enhance both system throughput and its stability. This adaptive mechanism alleviates the drawbacks of fixed parameter settings, improves flexibility in parameter selection, and significantly reduces optimization time, thereby improving the efficiency of antenna parameter tuning in mobile networks.

title: AI-Native Latency Optimization for 6G Industrial IoT: An Interpretable Machine Learning Approach to Robot Control Network Slicing
authors: Ruize Pang, Xuyang Zhang, Chenjun Ling, Mengchen Wang, Zhongmin Shao, Yuqian Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: The advent of 6G networks promises ultra-low latency communication essential for industrial automation, yet optimizing end-to-end delay in robot control systems remains challenging due to complex interdependencies between network and operational parameters. This paper presents a comprehensive machine learning framework for latency optimization in 6G enabled industrial environments. We decompose total communication delay into five components-command, feedback, data transfer, network, and processing delays-and employ Generalized Additive Models (GAM), SHAP analysis, and Partial Dependence Plots to identify critical bottlenecks. Experimental results reveal that data transfer time (33.36%), feedback delay (33.22%), and network latency (21.10%) dominate the delay budget. Our interpretable models achieve R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0. 9 6 with MAE of 0.87 ms, while uncovering optimal operational envelopes where robot speeds of 0.4-0.6 m/s minimize latency. These findings enable data-driven network slice configuration for real-time industrial IoT applications.

title: EDC-YOLO: A Maritime Ship Detection Method with Adaptive Gating and Content-Guided Fusion
authors: Weihong Lin, Shengjie Xie
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: To address the key challenges in maritime ship detection, including multi-scale ship targets, strong background noise interference, and proneness to false detection and missed detection, an improved detection model EDC-YOLO based on YOLOv8n is proposed. The model first introduces the C2f-EMA module to enhance feature extraction capability. Secondly, a Dual-branch Adaptive Gated Aggregation (DAGA) module is designed and deployed at the backbone-neck fusion stage, utilizing parallel coordinate attention methods with average and maximum dynamic fusion to enhance adaptability to complex noise and detection stability. Furthermore, a Content-Guided Feature Fusion (CGFF) module is constructed to dynamically fuse backbone-extracted and neck-output features through content guidance, enhancing local spatial modeling capability. Experimental results on our self-built dataset demonstrate that the improved model achieves a precision of 94.35%, recall of 84.26%, and mAP @ 0.5 of 93.01%, representing improvements of 2.79%, 1.48%, and 1.92% respectively over the YOLOv8n model. The improved model demonstrates enhanced detection accuracy, making it well-suited for practical maritime ship detection requirements.

title: Total-Editing: Head Avatar with Editable Appearance, Motion, and Lighting
authors: Yizhou Zhao, Chunjiang Liu, Haoyu Chen, Bhiksha Raj, Min Xu, Tadas Baltrušaitis, Mitch Rundle, HsiangTao Wu, Kamran Ghasedi
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Face reenactment and portrait relighting are essential tasks in portrait editing, yet they are typically addressed independently, without much synergy. Most face reenactment methods prioritize motion control and multiview consistency, while portrait relighting focuses on adjusting shading effects. To take advantage of both geometric consistency and illumination awareness, we introduce Total-Editing, a unified portrait editing framework that enables precise control over appearance, motion, and lighting. Specifically, we design a neural radiance field decoder with intrinsic decomposition capabilities. This allows seamless integration of lighting information from portrait images or HDR environment maps into synthesized portraits. We also incorporate a moving least squares based deformation field to enhance the spatiotemporal coherence of avatar motion and shading effects. With these innovations, our unified framework significantly improves the quality and realism of portrait editing results. Further, the multi-source nature of Total-Editing supports more flexible applications, such as illumination transfer from one portrait to another, or portrait animation with customized backgrounds.

title: Multi-Scale Hybrid CNN-Transformer for Smoke Detection in Satellite Images
authors: Tony Zhang, Robert Dick
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of smoke detection from satellite images, which is crucial for identifying and mitigating wildfires. Smoke can vary in size, shape, and texture, making it difficult to classify using remote sensing images. While several CNN architectures have been proposed for smoke detection, they have limitations in modeling long-range context in images due to convolution's bias towards learning local relationships. To address this limitation, the paper describes a hybrid network that combines CNN and transformer architectures. The transformer architecture leverages multi-head attention to learn long-range, global relationships among different image regions. Initially, multi-scale features are extracted by adding the transformer architecture after each CNN layer. Additionally, another transformer layer is appended to capture relationships among features in different receptive fields, significantly improving model accuracy. The proposed approach is evaluated on the USTC_SmokeRS dataset of remote sensing images for smoke detection and outperforms prior methods.

title: The Role of Social Network Sites in Raising Awareness of Sustainability Among Communities
authors: Esraa Hijah, Mohammad Alhassan, Ayman Alswaitti
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Social Networks Analysis, Management and Security (SNAMS)
abstract: This paper emphasizes the significant role of social network sites (SNSs) in promoting the sustainability development goals and environmental awareness among local communities. Sustainability is becoming one of the major concepts and criteria to be adopted and implemented in all daily activities of organizations and communities. Similarly, the platforms of SNSs are becoming a huge part of the communities’ daily lives. People from different educational and social backgrounds are using the SNSs platforms. This is giving an enormous importance to their roles in promoting relevant concepts and raising awareness. This research study was completed based on the results of a survey that addressed various community members to observe the power of the information and data shared on SNSs in educating local communities regarding sustainability by spreading awareness among them. The findings of the study proved that the SNSs are an extremely powerful tool to instruct people about the sustainability concepts and objectives. Approximately $75 \%$ of the participants believed that the SNSs platforms have positively influenced their own personal awareness and actions related to sustainability. Almost $53 \%$ stated that their purchasing decisions have been impacted by sustainability-related information they found on the SNSs. In addition, approximately $84 \%$ of the participants took sustainability-related actions in their daily lives due to the awareness and information shared on the SNSs platforms.

title: Potential Uses and Limitations of AI in Cancer and Infectious Disease Assessment Diagnostics: A Quick Reference
authors: Tanushree Budhbaware, Jaishriram Rathored, Mohd Basheeruddin, Sana Qausain
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: This review explores the prospective applications and constraints of artificial intelligence (AI) in infectious disease and cancer diagnostics, emphasizing its potential for early detection, precision diagnosis, and personalized treatment planning. The study analyzed AI methodologies like machine learning, deep learning, and natural language processing, and their integration with digital health technologies, focusing on their applications in infectious disease management and oncology. AI demonstrates superior sensitivity and specificity across multiple diagnostic domains, facilitating rapid pathogen identification, outbreak prediction, and cancer lesion detection. Integration with next-generation sequencing enables detection of actionable mutations and risk profiling. Digital health integration through wearables, telemedicine, and electronic health records expands accessibility. Predictive modeling platforms like BlueDot and HealthMap illustrate AI's role in real-time surveillance. AI adoption faces challenges like data bias, interpretability, regulatory uncertainties, and infrastructure constraints. Explicit AI and federated training address privacy, reduce prejudice, and foster trust, while clinical integration requires standardized procedures. AI has great potential to transform the diagnosis of cancer and infectious diseases by providing quick, precise, and scalable solutions. Ensuring fair access and long-term adoption in various healthcare systems will need international collaboration to remove ethical, technological, and regulatory obstacles.

title: Distributed incremental saturation kernel-based correntropy filter subject to unknown non-Gaussian noise and measurement bias
authors: Jingang Liu, Shenmin Song
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In this paper, a distributed incremental saturation correntropy filter is proposed for multi-sensor network under unknown non-Gaussian noise and measurement bias. First, to suppress non-Gaussian noise, a saturation kernel function with nonlinear compression characteristics is developed, and its properties are investigated. It combines the benefits of both Gaussian and Cauchy kernel functions. Second, an incremental measurement equation is constructed by the difference method to eliminate the unknown measurement bias. Concurrently, an adaptive noise variance estimator is designed based on the incremental measurement innovation, enabling real-time estimation of unknown incremental noise variance. Subsequently, the incremental saturation correntropy filter is derived by the presented saturation correntropy criterion, with a dynamic parameter adjustment strategy introduced to mitigate parameter selection uncertainty. The improved covariance intersection technique is then employed to achieve the consensus estimation of the whole sensor network. In addition, the unbiasedness, boundedness and convergence of the proposed algorithms are rigorously established under appropriate conditions. Finally, the simulation results of three scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed algorithms.

title: Dual-view correlation entropy filter for enhanced mechanical prognosis under non-Gaussian interference
authors: Minqiang Deng, Aidong Deng, Meng Xu, Wenbin Bian, Yang Liu, Dongying Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: State vector update based on Kalman filtering (KF) has significantly improved the adaptability of stochastic degradation modeling in mechanical prognosis. However, traditional research relies on the second-order moment of state observation deviation, which faces two challenges in practical applications. On the one hand, the indirect correlation between state observations and model parameters reduces the efficiency of parameter calibration. On the other hand, the absence of high-order statistical information makes the traditional update mechanism vulnerable to non-Gaussian noise, such as impulsive noise and outliers. To address these issues, a novel dual-view correlation entropy filter (DVCEF) is proposed to enhance the prognostic performance under non-Gaussian interference, which integrates two innovations. Firstly, a dual-view degradation observation (DVDO) strategy incorporating the optimal drift coefficient estimation is established to directly guide the parameter update. Then, a correlation entropy filtering (CEF) algorithm is proposed, which introduces the correlation entropy criterion to improve the robustness to irregular fluctuations. Finally, with the updated degradation parameters, the RUL distribution is derived through Bayesian inference. Experimental results on three run-to-failure datasets indicate that the proposed DVCEF can effectively enhance the prognostic performance. The average cumulative relative accuracy reaches 79.54%, 89.11%, and 80.91% respectively. Comparison with state-of-the-art works validates the superiority of this approach.

title: Digital twins in forest management using scalable Deep Learning Pipeline
authors: Mohamed Islam KESKES, Mihai Daniel Niţă
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The integration of diverse forestry datasets, including ground inventories, LiDAR scans, and high-resolution remote sensing imagery—presents considerable challenges for effective data processing and model generalization. This study presents an automated deep learning framework that predicts four key forest stand attributes—volume (Vol), basal area (BA), diameter at breast height (DBH), and tree height (H)—using Sentinel-2 satellite imagery. The framework was tested in Romania and Finland, utilizing self-optimizing neural architectures tuned with the Hyperband algorithm and enhanced by autoencoder-based feature extraction to improve robustness and reduce redundancy. Ground data in Romania were collected via the Arboreal mobile app, while Finnish data came from MS-NFI datasets. The model was initially trained on Romanian data and later improved by adding Finnish data, increasing predictive accuracy and adaptability. Hyperband tuning adjusted model depth based on attribute complexity, with deeper architectures used for Vol and BA, and shallower ones for DBH and H. Autoencoders performed better with larger datasets, reducing reconstruction errors and improving latent space representation, as confirmed by t-SNE visualizations showing distinct clustering. Performance improved significantly with Finnish data integration, notably boosting R² values (e.g., Vol in Finland from 0.483 to 0.718; H in Romania from 0.604 to 0.747) and lowering error metrics. The study offers a scalable, adaptable deep learning solution for forestry management, enhancing accuracy across diverse ecosystems.

title: Improving Surface Property Retrievals in Boreal Seasonal Snowpacks through Multiscale Modeling of Subgrid Reflectance
authors: Siddharth Singh, Ana P. Barros
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Time-series of surface reflectance and derived land cover and snowpack properties, including snow grain size (SGS), Normalized Difference Snow Index (NDSI), and Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index were analyzed across multiple spatial resolutions using Landsat (30 m), MODIS (500 m), and VIIRS (1 km) observations over a 10-year period in the Western Canadian domain of NASA's Arctic and Boreal Vulnerability Experiment (ABoVE). The objective is to demonstrate a scalable framework for decomposing VIIRS reflectance signals and associated surface property estimates by accounting for the subgrid-scale heterogeneity introduced by mixed landcover types such as forests, wetlands, and open snow fields. The approach is to separate snow and vegetation reflectance contributions within coarse-resolution pixels and leverage this information toward improving retrieval of SGS, NDSI, and SAVI. The analysis showed that differences between VIIRS 1 km coarse scale reflectance and Landsat averaged reflectance at the same resolution are closely linked to subgrid-scale variability in forest fraction, spatial organization of forests (quantified by pixel-based chi square heterogeneity), and the spectral behavior of the subgrid 500 m MODIS and VIIRS bands. To address these discrepancies across scales, a random forest model was trained against high-resolution Landsat data to predict separately the mean and standard deviation of subgrid reflectance within each VIIRS pixel for forested and non-forested areas using VIIRS observations and ancillary data (topography and land-cover). Model results show strong agreement with Landsat for mean NIR reflectance (RMSE < 0.07, R² > 0.90) and good skill in capturing spatial variability (R² ∼ 0.5-0.7), particularly in heterogeneous forested regions. Predicted reflectance distributions were used to estimate NDSI, SAVI, and SGS, demonstrating improved agreement with Landsat-derived values and reducing known biases in VIIRS-based retrievals.

title: Robust Hierarchical Traveling-Wave Partial Discharge Localization for Efficient Insulation Defect Detection of Medium-Voltage Overhead Lines
authors: Yuan Yan, Lei Jin, Junbai Chen, Yang Yang, Hongjie Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Medium-voltage overhead lines typically span kilometers, yet classical partial discharge (PD) detection methods are limited to meter-scale ranges, resulting in inefficient inspection. Traveling-wave PD localization provides a kilometer-scale monitoring solution that addresses this fundamental mismatch in spatial scale. However, its field deployment is hindered by insufficient detector sensitivity and severe reliability degradation caused by noise, frequency-dependent attenuation, and wave reflections in complex line environments. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a “global-to-local” detection methodology that enables both large-scale screening and equipment-level identification through hierarchical time-difference-of-arrival and polarity analysis. A dedicated detector has been developed, incorporating a non-invasive high-frequency current transformer and an ultra-low-noise amplifier capable of resolving sub-100 μA PD pulses, all synchronized via the Global Navigation Satellite System for multi-terminal acquisition. A dual-domain processing framework further enhances system robustness by integrating autocorrelation-guided reflection suppression in the time domain with adaptive subband fusion in the frequency domain, compensating for frequency-dependent attenuation while suppressing interference. Validation through simulation, laboratory tests, and field deployment confirms accurate defect localization under realistic noise and reflection conditions, demonstrating the practical viability of the system.

title: Real-Time ABR Signal Classification Using Machine Learning Techniques: A LabVIEW-Based Implementation
authors: Metin Taşkin, İlhan Umut
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR) testing is a cornerstone of auditory and neurological diagnostics, providing objective evaluation of auditory pathways. Despite its widespread clinical use, traditional manual ABR signal classification methods are time-consuming, prone to human error, and dependent on highly skilled personnel. This study proposes a framework for ABR signal classification leveraging advanced machine learning techniques integrated within the LabVIEW environment. By combining Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) based feature extraction with machine learning models---Support Vector Machines (SVM), Logistic Regression, and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)---the proposed system achieves up to 95.42% classification accuracy. The hardware-software architecture, utilizing National Instruments (NI) myRIO for embedded processing, provides automated signal acquisition and classification capabilities. This automated system significantly enhances diagnostic efficiency, reduces reliance on manual interpretation, and provides scalable solutions adaptable to diverse clinical and research needs. These findings underscore the transformative potential of machine learning in ABR diagnostics, paving the way for improved early detection of auditory and cognitive disorders.

title: Development and Comparison of Bare Soil Moisture Retrieval Methods for Compact Polarimetric Data
authors: Bhanu Prakash Mookkuthala Erkaramana, Thomas Jagdhuber, Kalifa Goïta, Ramata Magagi, Anke Fluhrer, Florian Hellwig, Hongquan Wang, G. G. Ponnurangam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Retrieval of soil moisture is crucial for weather and climate change predictions as well as in decision-making for agriculture management practices. Several theoretical, semi-empirical, empirical as well as machine learning-based scattering models have been developed for simulating fully polarimetric (FP) Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data. The potential of SAR Compact Polarimetry (CP) for soil moisture retrieval over bare agriculture fields is still an active area of research. In this study, we develop new CP backscattering models for soil moisture retrieval under bare soil conditions. Firstly, we apply empirical relationships between FP and CP to formulate an empirical CP-Advanced Integral Equation Model (AIEM). In addition, we propose two adapted theoretical models CP-AIEM and CP-Improved Integral Equation Model (CP-I2EM) using the direct analytical relationship between FP and CP. We also propose two empirical models CP-Dubois and CP-Oh by recalibrating Dubois and Oh models using CP observations and in-situ data. To compare the soil moisture retrieval performance of developed CP-backscattering models with that of a standard machine learning approach, the Random Forest (RF) technique is utilized. The six adapted and developed algorithms are tested using the C-band CP data of Canada’s RADARSAT Constellation Mission (RCM). In-situ soil moisture, roughness and texture measurements were collected from bare agriculture fields of Lennoxville and Montérégie, Quebec, Canada for calibration and validation of models. Results show that the RF model provides accurate estimates (error: RMSE = 0.04 m3/m3, correlation: r = 0.8 and inversion rate: IR = 100%) while requiring extensive training. CP-Oh and CP-Dubois models perform adequately (RMSE = 0.08 m3/m3, r>0.7 and IR>60%) having limited applicability range. In the end, CP-AIEM offers the best choice including reasonable accuracy (RMSE = 0.07 m3/m3, r = 0.6 and IR = 64%), wider applicability range and transferability without requiring calibration.

title: Sensor Networked Resilient Distributed State Estimation With Cyber Attacks: Theory and Application
authors: Zheng Zhang, Xiwang Dong, Wenrui Ding, Xiaoduo Li, Zhang Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: In this article, a sensor networked resilient distributed estimation framework has been proposed, where intercommunication in the network is susceptible to cyber-attacks. Considering the uncertainty of the maneuvering characteristics of the target, an improved dynamic model is established for calculating the accurate local priori results by introducing a jerk component. Based on the consensus fusion protocol, the final estimation results are obtained after local neighboring interactions. To mitigate the impact of cyber-attacks on the intercommunication between neighboring nodes, the DoS-attacks are modeled with its average frequency and duration. In light of the target maneuvering model and DoS-attacks model, the resilient distributed algorithm is designed by local filtering, resilient, and consensus fusion stages. Additionally, the stability of the proposed resilient distributed estimation algorithm is analyzed with global observability. The simulation results demonstrate the performance of the proposed resilient algorithm. Furthermore, the cooperative equivalent experiment is conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm under DoS-attacks, which is consistent with the simulation results.

title: On the Ergodic Rate of Reconfigurable Optical Wireless Communication Systems
authors: Apostolos A. Tegos, Sotiris A. Tegos, Panagiotis D. Diamantoulakis
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: In this work, we investigate an optical wireless communication network, consisting of a transmitter with multiple elements and a randomly deployed user. For this network, we derive a novel closed-form expression for the lower bound of the ergodic rate (ER) for three unique scenarios. These scenarios include a static system, where the orientation of both components is fixed, a partially reconfigurable system, where either the transmitter or the receiver orientation can be optimized, and finally a fully reconfigurable system, where both orientations can be optimized. The simulation results demonstrate the impact of various simulation parameters on the performance of the system and prove that the derived ER using the lower-bound is a great approximation of the actual ER.

title: Multi-View MIMO Radar Forward-Looking Imaging under Impulsive Noise via Low-Rank and Adaptive Pattern-Coupled Variational Bayesian Learning
authors: Jiadong Yi, Minglei Yang, Nan Liu, Yu Chen, Meng Liu, Francesco Soldovieri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Conventional radar forward-looking imaging methods often suffer from degraded performance due to limited angular diversity and strong noise interference. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a multi-view multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar imaging model that incorporates radar cross section (RCS) fluctuations and leverages low-rank approximation (LRA) to optimize the radiation field reference matrix. This method can effectively mitigate matrix ill-conditioning, thereby enhancing imaging stability and robustness against noise. Furthermore, an adaptive pattern-coupled variational Bayesian learning (APC-VBL) algorithm is introduced, which captures spatial dependencies among neighboring scatterers through pattern coupling and dynamically adjusts coupling parameters based on target characteristics. The VBL framework is employed to perform target reconstruction and parameter estimation under impulsive noise, thereby enhancing structural fidelity and resilience to interference. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances imaging quality and target recognition accuracy under multi-view configurations, validating its effectiveness for radar forward-looking imaging in complex noise conditions.

title: Fairness Optimization in Beyond Diagonal STAR-RIS-aided NOMA-Enhanced NF-ISAC
authors: Rahul Prakash Singh, Keshav Singh, Sandeep Kumar Singh, Yatindra Nath Singh
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This paper explores fairness optimization in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems supported by beyond diagonal simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (BD-STAR-RIS) combined with non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) in near field (NF) channel model scenarios. An ISAC fairness maximization problem is formulated by jointly designing the transmit beamforming at the base station (BS) and the BD-STAR-RIS coefficient matrices. We propose an alternating optimization-based algorithm that tackles the non-convex nature of the problem and provides an optimal solution using tools such as successive convex approximation (SCA), semidefinite programming (SDP) for joint transmit and phase beamforming, and Rayleigh quotient method for receive beamforming. We validate the proposed algorithm’s convergence, accuracy, and effectiveness through Monte Carlo simulations, demonstrating the advantages of NOMA based BD-STAR-RIS in near field ISAC systems. Finally, the BD-STAR-RIS-NOMA aided ISAC achieves better fairness compared to the benchmark technologies.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems

title: GMNV: Dynamic Quantile-Augmented K-Means Clustering with Variance-Based Feature Selection
authors: Maradana Durga Venkata Prasad, Srikanth Thota
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The Global Minimum Nearest Value (GMNV) algorithm—where "Global" denotes consideration of all samples for quantile mapping, and "Minimum" refers to assigning each value to its nearest quantile—enhances K-Means clustering by augmenting the feature set with a quantile-based feature derived from the highest-variance feature, mapped to dynamic quantiles q1, q2, . . . , qη. Applied to the Iris, Wine, and Breast Cancer datasets using three selected features (s = 3), GMNV optimizes quantiles via grid search. Averaged over 10 runs, GMNV consistently outperforms standard K-Means on Silhouette Scores (Iris: 0.56.36 vs. 0.5405; Wine: 0.36.46 vs. 0.3539; Breast Cancer: 0.74.14 vs. 0.6857), Accuracy (e.g., Breast Cancer: 0.9033 vs. 0.8313), and Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) (e.g., Breast Cancer: 0.6461 vs. 0.4256). Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE), applied post-clustering to both baseline and GMNV-augmented data, serve dual purposes: visualization and qualitative verification of enhanced clustering accuracy through significantly improved cluster separation and cohesion before versus after GMNV augmentation—most prominently in t-SNE projections. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis highlights the quantile feature’s critical contribution, whileWilcoxon signed-rank tests confirm statistical significance of improvements across all datasets. GMNV offers a robust, lightweight solution for noisy, high-dimensional data with strong performance across diverse structures.

title: SimGroup-FS: Multi-Way Redundancy Modeling for Interpretable Feature Selection in Supervised Learning
authors: Mohammad Hossein Safarpour
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Feature selection is a cornerstone of high-dimensional supervised learning, critically influencing model accuracy, computational efficiency, and interpretability. Conventional filter-based methods, however, predominantly rely on univariate relevance or pairwise redundancy measures, which are inherently limited in capturing complex, multi-way dependencies among three or more features. To address this gap, we propose SimGroup-FS, a graph-theoretic feature selection framework that explicitly models structural redundancy through connected component analysis. The method constructs a cosine-similarity graph over the feature set, applies a similarity threshold ϵ to define edges, and identifies redundancy groups as connected components. From each group, a single representative feature is selected based on its discriminative relevance to the target variable, using flexible metrics such as Spearman correlation, mutual information, or ANOVA F-values. Evaluated on six diverse UCI benchmark datasets—including hybrid and purely numerical types—SimGroup-FS achieves an average dimensionality reduction of 88.8%, with reductions exceeding 96% on datasets like wdbc and ionosphere. Crucially, classification performance is consistently preserved or significantly enhanced across multiple classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, and k-NN). Most notably, on the wdbc dataset, the SVM F1-score improves from a near-failure level of 0.019 to a robust 0.887, demonstrating the method’s ability to mitigate noise-induced model degradation. SimGroup-FS operates in O(m2n) time, is model-agnostic, fully reproducible, and yields interpretable feature groupings.

title: Self-Adjusting Feed-Forward Ripple Cancellation in a Hybrid GaN/Si Interleaved Inverter
authors: Mowei Lu, Jurgis Reinotas, Xiaoyang Tian, Yejing Fan, Nima Tashakor, Florin Udrea, Stefan M. Goetz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: In hybrid interleaved wide-bandgap (WBG)/silicon (Si) structures, the Si part could shoulder most of the load, while WBG contributes only fractionally and primarily cancels out the current ripple of the Si bridge. However, such a mode required perfectly known inductances of each bridge. It also needed accurate ripple measurement and fast closed loop control with extreme demands on the sensing, filtering, and control bandwidth. Additionally, previous use was limited to dc conversion, where the quasi-stationary output voltage simplifies operation. This paper proposes a novel control strategy for a hybrid GaN/Si interleaved inverter, which uses low bandwidth feed-back control for fundamental current sharing in combination with a feed-forward term for ripple cancellation. This approach decouples high-frequency current ripple from low frequency fundamental currents and achieves fast response and efficient ripple cancellation without the excessive demands of full bandwidth feedback control. Furthermore, we introduce a self adjusting or auto-tuning algorithm. It dynamically updates the control coefficient through real-time ripple indicators to adapt to inductance variations and ensure optimal ripple cancellation.

title: Enhanced Offline Identification for Induction Motors at Standstill: Overcoming Nonlinearity and Enabling Multi-Mode Cooperation
authors: Ying Wang, Bo Wang, Dianguo Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Accurate electrical parameters are essential for high-performance control of induction motors (IM). However, conventional parameter identification methods suffer from limited accuracy and adaptability due to motor structural asymmetry, inverter nonlinearity, and the time-varying characteristic of IM parameters. To address these issues, this paper proposes a standstill identification method considering the nonlinearity effect, which employs the phase-specific multi-frequency current signal injection. The studied strategy addresses three key challenges in the existing schemes. First, an adaptive voltage error elimination method is introduced to suppress the effects of inverter nonlinearity. Second, a multi-frequency excitation signal injection strategy is developed to decouple the identification of IM parameters. Third, a DC-biased AC excitation scheme is incorporated to accurately identify mutual inductance under different magnetic saturation conditions, thereby enabling multi-mode cooperative control. Finally, the experimental validation on a 2.2-kW IM drive system shows the accuracy and feasibility of the studied method. This paper is accompanied by a video that demonstrates the entire process of the proposed parameter identification method.

title: A Compact PCB-based Strong-Coupling Resonator for Oscillation Suppression of SiC MOSFETs
authors: Yun-Hui Mei, Lu Wang, Yuqing Cui, Zhanpeng Liu, Gaojia Zhu, Zetian Yu, Chuyuan Liu, Longnv Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The high-speed switching of silicon carbide (SiC) power devices is often accompanied by voltage/current overshoot and high-frequency oscillation caused by circuit parasitics. Conventional solutions, such as RC snubber circuits or active gate drivers, often come at the cost of increased switching losses or system complexity. Meanwhile, RLC resonators face a trade-off between compactness and performance owing to weak coupling and large volume. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel strongly coupled resonator topology based on parity-time (PT) symmetric theory. The main breakthrough is the achievement of a coupling coefficient exceeding 0.4 within a compact 9 cm³ volume, ensuring efficient energy transfer and oscillation suppression at the physical level. This paper first constructs an RLC damping circuit with a strong-coupling topology, then integrates coupled-mode theory with circuit equations to establish a system energy model for guiding parameter design. Experimental results demonstrate a 77.15% reduction in turn‑off Vds, 77.78% in turn‑off Id, and 64.82% in turn‑on Id oscillations, with no significant additional loss. Compared to traditional methods, the proposed design provides improved oscillation suppression with low additional loss and compact size, offering a practical solution for high-speed SiC converters.

title: Integrating Microcontrollers and Data Analysis in Mechanics Education: A STEM Approach to Free Fall
authors: Jéssica Fabiana Mariano Dos Santos, Luiz Antônio De Oliveira Nunes
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: This article presents a STEM-based approach to teaching free fall by integrating Arduino, PLX-DAQ, and Excel. PLX-DAQ, originally an Excel add-in for serial communication with Arduino, was modified in this work, and these changes were important to enable full bidirectional communication. Through a custom-developed graphical interface in Excel, parameters can be sent to the Arduino, allowing experiment configurations to be adjusted in real time while also enabling efficient data acquisition and analysis. A photogate system was connected to an Arduino Uno, enabling precise measurement of the fall times of an acrylic bar of alternating stripes of different masses. The results show that the system enables highly accurate calculations of gravitational acceleration, with deviations of less than 2% from the expected values. This experimental setup offers educators a low-cost and effective tool to address common student misconceptions about free fall, while fostering a deeper understanding of the fundamental principles governing motion and reinforcing the role of the scientific method in physics education.

title: AI-OCPP Framework for Smart and Safe EV Charging: Real-World Performance Evaluation
authors: Md Sabbir Hossen, Gobbi Ramasamy, Ngu Eng Eng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) are changing how people use energy for transportation, and their growing adoption poses new challenges for safe and cost-effective charging operations. When multiple EVs charge simultaneously, aggregate demand can rise sharply, increasing the risk of grid overloads and high electricity costs. The Open Charge Point Protocol (OCPP) enables interoperability between charging stations and backend systems, but it does not provide built-in intelligence for forecasting or charging control. To address this gap, this paper presents a practical AI-driven OCPP framework that integrates short-term demand forecasting, tariff-aware reinforcement learning, and runtime safety enforcement into a unified control loop. The framework is evaluated using real OCPP logs and employs a tariff-aware, safety-constrained Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) scheduler that issues charging setpoints while respecting feeder and connector limits at every control step through a projection-based safety layer. A lightweight forecasting module provides short-horizon operational support aligned with the 15-minute control resolution, without being treated as a standalone predictive contribution. The proposed approach is evaluated under multiple tariff regimes, including flat rate, time-of-use (ToU), and real-time pricing (RTP), and compared against proportional fairness (PF) and rule-based baselines. Results show that when configured with explicit energy-parity constraints, the PPO controller maintains near-baseline energy delivery while achieving substantial reductions in peak demand and total energy cost. Across evaluated scenarios, peak demand is reduced by up to 30% and energy cost by approximately 25–33%, without violating grid safety constraints. The controller also demonstrates stable behavior across different pricing and loading conditions without extensive manual tuning. Overall, this study demonstrates that combining established learning methods with rigorous runtime safety enforcement and real-world validation can yield deployable and reliable AI-based charging control within OCPP-operated infrastructure. The framework emphasizes practical integration and deployment realism rather than algorithmic novelty, providing a viable pathway toward safer, more efficient, and cost-aware EV charging networks.

title: Efficient Message Authentication Scheme for IoT Networks Using Precoded-Tag Embedding
authors: Saud Althunibat, Raed Mesleh, Luae Al-Tarawneh
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Message authentication using tag embedding has been widely adopted for Internet of Things (IoT) networks due to its efficient performance and lower resource consumption compared to other tag appending approaches. Specifically, in tag embedding, the authentication tag and the message are superimposed in one signal to save time, energy, and spectral resources, making it an ideal alternative for networks with limited resources, such as IoT networks. However, due to the lower power allocated to the tag symbols (compared to the message symbols) in the conventional scheme, the received tag experiences higher transmission errors, increasing the authentication failure rate (AFR). Moreover, an Eve might be able to capture either the message, the tag or both as they are transmitted unprotected in the conventional scheme. In this work, a novel message authentication scheme is proposed to enhance the performance of the tag embedding scheme. Specifically, the proposed scheme involves precoding the tag signal prior to being superimposed with the message signal and emitted over the channel. The conducted analysis and evaluation reveal that the proposed scheme can significantly reduce both the tag error rate (TER) and the message error rate (MER), which should be directly reflected in improving the AFR. In addition, the performance at the eavesdropper is shown to be degraded due to the precoding of the tag signal induced in the proposed scheme. The included analysis and explored results investigate the impact of power allocation at the transmitter between the tag and message signals to minimize MER, TER or AFR.

title: Fast and Accurate Angle Estimation for MIMO Radar with a Virtual Non-Rectangular Array
authors: Maozhong Fu, Yishan Ye, Jun Tang, Zhenmiao Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Modern radar systems commonly utilize multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) architectures, which often result in non-rectangular virtual antenna arrays. For these non-rectangular configurations, conventional angle estimation methods based on uniform linear array (ULA) are suboptimal as they discard partial spatial information. Furthermore, subspace-based and iterative methods are often too computationally demanding for real-time applications. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a computationally efficient two-stage angle estimation method, termed monopulse on non-rectangular array (MONA), designed to fully exploit the entire virtual aperture. The first stage introduces a unique amplitude-normalized 2-dimensional (2D) discrete Fourier transform (DFT) process to generate a robust coarse angle estimate from the non-rectangular array data. Building on this, the second stage employs a statistically optimized amplitude comparison monopulse (ACM) technique to achieve off-grid, high-precision fine estimation. Theoretical analysis, simulations, and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high accuracy, approaching the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) while maintaining a low computational cost.

title:
Large-scale fabrication of fully printed, photoactivated Au decorated Tin Oxide based room-temperature NO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    sensors with ultra-high response on paper substrates
authors: Siri Chandana Amarakonda, Manvendra Singh, Mohammed Hadhi Pazhaya Puthanveettil, Subho Dasgupta
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Conventional metal oxide gas sensors require high-temperature operation (200-300°C), which limits integration with sustainable and biodegradable substrates and contributes to electronic waste. Herein, we demonstrate fully screen printed, room-temperature NO₂ sensors using UV-photoactivated Au decorated SnO/SnO₂ nanoparticles on paper. Tin oxide nanoparticles have been synthesized via precipitation and annealing, followed by controlled Au decoration (0.5 wt.%) through ascorbic acid reduction. Subsequently, screen printing enables scalable device fabrication with interdigitated Ag electrodes and Au-SnO/SnO₂ sensing layers on photo paper. Under 365 nm UV illumination (2.1 mW/cm²), the sensors have exhibited an excellent response of 871 for 3.2 ppm NO₂ at room temperature. The devices demonstrate remarkable sensitivity across 1.4-3.2 ppm level. Response and recovery times have been 253 s and 1120 s, respectively. Excellent selectivity toward NO₂ has been demonstrated against five interfering gases, with stable performance over repeated cycles. Successful integration into a portable USB-powered module has been demonstrated to showcase practical deployment capability. This approach offers a sustainable, scalable route for room-temperature gas sensing suitable for environmental and occupational health monitoring.

title: Semi-Supervised and Transfer Learning-Based Smart Contract Vulnerability Detection
authors: Hengjie Song, Yangkai Wang, Han Yu, Le Ma, Siyu Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Smart contracts are crucial for managing sensitive financial transactions. However, these contracts are inherently vulnerable which can cause significant security risks. Traditional methods for detecting smart contract vulnerabilities depend on expert-defined rules, which are often complicated and limited by human experts' individual experience. In contrast, deep learning-based approaches automatically extract intricate feature representations, greatly improving detection efficiency and accuracy. Nevertheless, these approaches rely on access to large amounts of high-quality labeled data. They are less effective when facing new types of vulnerabilities where labeled data are scarce. To this end, we propose the Semi-supervised Tuning (SST) approach for smart contract vulnerability detection. It first leverages a source model trained on labeled source data to extract features for new vulnerabilities. Subsequently, it performs semi-supervised learning to explore the feature structure of unlabeled data. In particular, SST groups contract code features and constructs a shared feature queue containing labeled and unlabeled contracts to explore the complete feature structure and guide model training. Extensive experimental evaluations based on two real-world datasets containing eleven smart contract vulnerabilities demonstrate that SST is significantly more advantageous compared to eight state-of-the-art baseline methods, outperforming them by 24.12% in terms of F1 scores.

title: UniFLE: Uniform Fusion of Multiple LoRA Experts for Backdoor Defense in Large Language Models
authors: Shuai Zhao, Qika Lin, Yanhao Jia, Xinyi Wu, Yuwen Li, Luu Anh Tuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Large language models (LLMs), which serve as a bridge between pre-training and task-specific adaptation, achieve state-of-the-art performance across several downstream tasks through full-parameter fine-tuning (FPFT). However, with the continuous growth of model parameter scales, FPFT requires substantial computational resources, which limits its practicality. Consequently, there is a growing shift toward parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods that update only a limited subset of model parameters, markedly reducing resource consumption. Although this paradigm fosters accelerated research advancements, it also introduces security risks. Empirical studies demonstrate that if LLM weights are backdoored, the backdoors can still be activated even after fine-tuning leveraging PEFT algorithms. To address the aforementioned issue, in this paper, we introduce a novel Uniform Fusion of multiple LoRA Experts algorithm, named UniFLE, designed to defend against backdoor attacks. Specifically, the UniFLE algorithm pioneers the insertion of multiple LoRA experts into the MLP blocks to expand the updatable feature subspace during fine-tuning, and fuses all experts to decouple backdoor features. Additionally, to enhance the diversity of LoRA experts, we introduce a diversity regularization loss that constrains correlations between different experts and encourages them to learn more novel features. The theoretical analysis demonstrates that the UniFLE algorithm effectively decreases the mutual information between the model's intermediate representations and the backdoor representations. To validate the effectiveness of the UniFLE algorithm, we conduct experiments across four tasks, four state-of-the-art LLMs, and three backdoor attack methods. The results consistently demonstrate that our UniFLE algorithm effectively defends against backdoor attacks while preserving model performance. We aspire for our method to enhance model security and contribute to the advancement of the LLM community.

title: A multi-class training dataset and hybrid neural network for simultaneous karst and channel detection
authors: Jiarun Yang, Xinming Wu, Guangyu Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Seismic karsts and channels interpretation is vital for reservoir characterization. These geobodies often coexist in seismic volumes and exhibit similar reflection features, making them challenging to distinguish using existing methods that typically interpret them separately. We propose a multi-class hybrid neural network for simultaneously detecting karsts and channels, offering more efficient and accurate results than independent detection. To address the lack of multi-class labeled data, we developed a workflow to generate a diverse synthetic training dataset. This involves constructing 3D impedance models characterized by stratigraphic sequences with karsts and channels embedded according to geological and empirical data. To simulate diverse models, key parameters such as velocities, dimensions, and orientations of geobodies are randomly assigned within reasonable ranges based on geological context. Reflectivity models are computed from impedance models and convolved with Ricker wavelets to simulate seismic data, with noise added to enhance realism, yielding diverse seismic volumes with multi-class labels. This dataset is used to train a U-shaped hybrid neural network combining a Swin-Transformer encoder with a residual module decoder. The Swin-transformer provides global context-awareness and captures long-range dependencies, while the decoder ensures detailed feature restoration. The network performs multi-class segmentation, simultaneously detecting and distinguishing karsts and channels. Field applications demonstrate that the trained model can detect these geobodies with high accuracy and integrity. From the detection, 3D meshes of the geobodies are constructed to model and analyze their structural geometries. We have made our multi-class training dataset publicly available (https://zenodo.org/records/10781510) for further research on karst and channel interpretation.

title: DuoCounter: An approach for counting fish using tracking
authors: Silva Souza, Eduardo Bedin, Vinícius de Araújo Maeda, Hemerson Pistori, Edson Takashi Matsubara
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Counting fingerlings remains a major challenge for fish farmers, as it demands both accuracy and efficiency to minimize errors and production costs. Automating this process through computer vision enables continuous monitoring and prevents duplicate counts by tracking individual fish. However, existing automated approaches that rely on delimiting a specific counting zone often suffer from identity switches. In many cases, fish are either missed or lost when passing through the zone and later re-identified after exiting and re-entering, leading to inaccuracies. To address these limitations, we propose DuoCounter, a tracking-based approach that integrates two reference lines to improve counting accuracy while maintaining a list of tracked objects and their corresponding keypoint-based features. These features are extracted and compared to resolve identity switches when they occur.
