The method was evaluated on real-world aquaculture videos with manually annotated ground truth, achieving a strong correlation with manual counts (0.97), as well as recall and precision scores of 0.91 and 0.83, respectively, using the YOLOv12 detector. The results demonstrate that DuoCounter effectively reduces identity switches, providing robust tracking and counting performance that matches manual counting while outperforming state-of-the-art methods

title: ST-DAGCN: Spatial-Temporal Directed Acyclic Graph Convolutional Networks for End-to-End Joint Effective Connectivity Learning and Resting-State fMRI Classification
authors: Rui Yang, Wenrui Dai, Huajun She, Yiping P. Du, Dapeng Wu, Hongkai Xiong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: Building comprehensive brain connectomes has proved of fundamental importance in resting-state fMRI (rs-fMRI) analysis. Based on the foundation of brain network, spatial-temporal-based graph convolutional networks have dramatically improved the performance of deep learning methods in rs-fMRI time series classification. However, existing works either pre-define the brain network as the correlation matrix derived from the raw time series or jointly learn the connectome and model parameters without any topology constraint. These methods could suffer from degraded classification performance caused by the deviation from the intrinsic brain connectivity and lack biological interpretability of demonstrating the causal structure (i.e., effective connectivity) among brain regions. Moreover, most existing methods for effective connectivity learning are unaware of the downstream classification task and cannot sufficiently exploit useful rs-fMRI label information. To address these issues in an end-to-end manner, we model the brain network as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) to discover direct causal connections between brain regions and propose Spatial-Temporal DAG Convolutional Network (ST-DAGCN) to jointly infer effective connectivity and classify rs-fMRI time series by learning brain representations based on nonlinear structural equation model. The optimization problem is formulated into a continuous program and solved with score-based learning method via gradient descent. We evaluate ST-DAGCN on three public rs-fMRI databases. Experiments show that ST-DAGCN outperforms existing models by evident margins in rs-fMRI classification and simultaneously learns meaningful edges of effective connectivity that help understand brain activity patterns and pathological mechanisms in brain disease.

title: Absorption enhancement mechanism of multi-frequency resonant terahertz metamaterial absorber and detection of forchlorfenuron
authors: Xiaoxue Du, Zhiwu Yu, Xiaoqian Du, Qiang Shi, Rong Fu, Yifan Guo, Yafei Wang, Xiaoyue Yang, Jun Sun
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The abuse of high concentrations of forchlorfenuron can lead to malformed fruits and pose risks to human health, highlighting the need for effective detection methods. In this study, a flexible multi-frequency resonant terahertz metamaterial sensor is designed and validated both theoretically and experimentally. The sensor exhibits magnetic dipole resonances at 0.53 THz and 2.30 THz, and the physical enhancement mechanism of the magnetic dipole resonances is analyzed through the distributions of electric field, magnetic field, and surface current. Simulation results reveal that the metamaterial sensor achieves a maximum detection sensitivity of 440 GHz/RIU, with multi-frequency resonance maintained across a polarization angle range of 0°– 90°. Experimental detection reveals that the designed multi-frequency resonant metamaterial sensor successfully detects different concentrations of forchlorfenuron, with a minimum detectable concentration of 0.01 mg/L. These theoretical and experimental results confirm the feasibility of a multi-frequency resonant terahertz metamaterial sensor for forchlorfenuron detection.

title: Landtagswahlen in Saxony and Thuringia: Empirical Framework for Measuring Populism in Electoral Manifestos
authors: André Schmale
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Social Networks Analysis, Management and Security (SNAMS)
abstract: This case study examines 14 electoral manifestos of a total of seven parties for the state elections in Saxony and Thuringia in Germany in 2024. It does so on the basis of democratic theory and classical theories of political representation. The objective is to present a procedure for identifying qualitative differences in election programs using quantitative text analysis methods. In particular, the focus is on developing a model to determine or measure the degree of populist language based on a dictionary. In the final step, the article discusses the extent to which it is possible and useful to use the model as a starting point for classifying election programs.

title: Partial Discharge Localization Based on Direct Data-Reusing TDE Algorithm With Generalized Cauchy Loss
authors: Qiang Qin, Lu Lu, Guangya Zhu, Tao Lei, Kai Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: The presence of impulsive noise can significantly compromise the localization accuracy of partial discharge (PD). To address this problem, the direct data-reusing time-delay estimation (TDE) algorithm with the generalized Cauchy loss (GCL) function (DDR-TDE-GCL) is proposed. Leveraging the robustness of the GCL function, the proposed algorithm demonstrates a superior capability to combat impulsive interference. In addition, the direct TDE method is integrated into the data-reusing strategy. The integration enables the DDR-TDE-GCL algorithm to achieve high localization accuracy in cable systems while remaining noniterative. Moreover, the convergence behavior of the DDR-TDE-GCL algorithm is analyzed, proving that the estimation for the attenuation factor is unbiased and consistent. Simulations and experiments demonstrate that the DDR-TDE-GCL algorithm has an improved localization accuracy compared to existing algorithms for PD signals, achieving an average localization accuracy of 99.59%.

title: Magnol.AI Copilot: Multimodal LLMs for Natural, Accessible Data Interaction
authors: Guangchen Ruan, Hui Zhang, Hui Xiao, Jian Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Magnol.Ai Conversational dBM Analysis Tool, an innovative approach to transforming interactions with the Magnol.Ai platform. Leveraging advanced large language models (LLMs) and a multi-agent architecture, the tool addresses the challenges of navigating complex digital clinical trial data by enabling researchers to query and analyze datasets through intuitive natural language interactions. The system integrates robust natural language understanding, intelligent query handling, and seamless interoperability with existing platform functionalities. These capabilities streamline workflows, reduce the complexity of data exploration, and empower scientists to derive actionable insights more efficiently. Designed for scalability and adaptability, the tool meets the evolving needs of clinical research environments, enhancing the accessibility and utility of digital biomarkers (dBM) data. By demonstrating the potential of conversational AI in advancing scientific workflows, this work highlights the transformative impact of combining state-of-the-art AI technologies with domain-specific applications in digital biomarker research.

title: Advancing Lung Disease Diagnosis in 3D CT Scans
authors: Qingqiu Li, Runtian Yuan, Junlin Hou, Jilan Xu, Yuejie Zhang, Rui Feng, Hao Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: To enable more accurate diagnosis of lung disease in chest CT scans, we propose a straightforward yet effective model. Firstly, we analyze the characteristics of 3D CT scans and remove non-lung regions, which helps the model focus on lesion-related areas and reduces computational cost. We adopt ResNeSt-50 as a strong feature extractor, and use a weighted cross-entropy loss to mitigate class imbalance, especially for the minority squamous cell carcinoma category. Based on this, we further incorporate mixup and contrastive learning to improve the model's accuracy and robustness. Our model achieves a Macro F1 score of 84.29 on the validation set and 70.40 on the test set, and wins the first place in the Fair Disease Diagnosis Challenge.

title: Classifications between Different Stages of Alzheimer's Disease using Xtreme Gradient Boosting
authors: Nafis Adnan Mondal, Rajdeep Senapati, Soumyajit Biswas, Abhijit Chandra
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th Conference on Applied Signal Processing (ASPCON)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease, abbreviated as AD, is a progressively debilitating ailment resulting in memory loss and deterioration of cognitive functions affecting millions of people worldwide. Therefore, it is very important to identify the early stages of AD accurately and effectively for timely intervention and management. This research has employed Xtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) which is a powerful machine learning algorithm to classify various stages of AD using MRI brain scans obtained from Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) database. Present study focuses on three core classification tasks viz. Healthy Control vs Early Mild Cognitive Impairment, Late Mild Cognitive Impairment vs Early Mild Cognitive Impairment, and Late Mild Cognitive Impairment vs AD. Notably, this study identified significant differences in terms of several brain tissue parameters through z-tests analysis which were subsequently used during training of each classifier task on XGBoost. Based on the findings of the proposed work, it has been concluded that XGBoost model can accurately segregate various stages of AD by utilizing the statistically significant variables.

title: A Study on Resistive Force and Torque Feedback from Muscles and Tissue in Spine Surgical Robots for Improved Precision and Control
authors: Panuwat Oiamwong, Sorayouth Chumnanvej, Jackrit Suthakorn
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: Minimally invasive spinal surgery (MISS) faces significant challenges due to unknown resistance forces from muscles and tissues, adversely affecting screw fixation accuracy. This study characterizes force and torque responses to improve spinal surgical robot control and precision. The proposed system employs a newly designed instrument tip with a 6 -axis force/torque sensor and cylindrical guidance sleeve, aligned with the spinal screw insertion point. Experiments used a multilayered lumbar spine phantom fabricated from biomimetic silicone replicating muscle and soft tissue properties. Raw data underwent gravity compensation and signal filtering for stability and accuracy. Results show the phantom effectively reproduces tissue elasticity, with peak resistance forces varying by position and direction (left L1: Roll <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5. 6 7} \mathbf{N}$</tex>, Pitch <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5. 3 2}$</tex> N). Forces and torques exhibited linear relationships with inclination angle and insertion depth, where small angular deviations at greater depths induce excessive resistance. Stiffer silicone phantoms produced higher resistance forces. These findings highlight the necessity for adaptive robotic control strategies accommodating patient-specific biomechanical variations, providing foundation for force/torque compensation algorithms and optimized robotic motion planning to enhance MISS precision, safety, and stability.

title: Research on Switching Loss Optimization of SiC Inverters with Improved Model Predictive Control
authors: Wenshang Xu
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Grid and Energy Engineering (SGEE)
abstract: In high-performance servo drives and precision motion control systems, a shortened control cycle improves dynamic response. However, increased switching frequency leads to higher switching losses, which constrains system efficiency and aggravates thermal management challenges. The field-oriented control (FOC) combined with space vector pulse width modulation (SVPWM) is traditionally used. This method faces challenges in balancing dynamic performance and loss control under high-frequency conditions. An optimized strategy based on finite control set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) is proposed. A switching action penalty term is explicitly incorporated into the cost function. Dynamic constraint of switching behavior and effective suppression of system losses are achieved. A simulation platform using a SiC MOSFET three-phase inverter is established. Verification is carried out with a resistive-inductive load. Results show that compared with the traditional FOC strategy, the proposed improved MPC strategy maintains excellent transient response performance. A significant reduction in switching losses is also achieved. A feasible solution is provided for high-bandwidth and high-efficiency power electronic conversion systems.

title: Enhancing Breast Cancer Diagnosis Using Lightweight Deep Learning Models
authors: Jordan Mozebo, Xiaohong Yuan, Madhuri Siddula, Olusola Odeyomi
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Breast cancer remains one of the leading causes of mortality among women worldwide, underscoring the need for early and accurate detection methods. This study investigates the use of deep learning for breast cancer detection, with a focus on lightweight models to improve accuracy. Although there are various imaging techniques exist, this research specifically uses histopathological images from the BreakHis and IDC dataset; and a MRI dataset. Advanced deep learning models, including MobileNetV3-Small, BCDNet, ShuffleNetV2, and EfficientNet-B0 are evaluated. Five-fold cross-validation was applied during training to ensure reliable and unbiased performance estimation, reduce overfitting risk, and provide stable performance. Experimental results show that MobileNetV3-Small and EfficientNet-B0 consistently achieved the highest testing and validatiion accuracy across all three datasets (BreakHis, IDC, and MRI), when evaluated with 5-fold cross-validation. On the BreakHis dataset, MobileNetV3-Small achieved a testing and validation accuracy of 99.62% and 98.62%, respectively. On IDC dataset, MobileNetV3-Small achieved 94.26% testing accuracy and 93.06% validation accuracy. On MRI dataset, MobileNetV3-Small achieved 99.53% testing accuracy and 99.23% validation accuracy. Our findings highlight the promise of lightweight deep learning models in histopathological and MRI image analysis, offering reliable solutions for early breast cancer detection while reducing computational costs. This study emphasizes the value of streamlined architectures in improving the accuracy of medical diagnosis, thus contributing to better patient outcomes.

title: Development of a Hybrid prediction model for Drowsiness Monitoring
authors: Dipika Pradhan, Priyanka Bhuttapalle, Kalyani Praveen Wagh, Neeta Kadam
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: In order to reduce traffic accidents, it is critical to identify sleepy drivers. This research presents a reliable method that uses convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Haar cascade classifiers to accurately and instantly identify driver weariness. One of the main factors influencing traffic accidents is driver drowsiness, which highlights the significance of constantly checking driver attentiveness. To identify eye closure as a sign of tiredness, the suggested approach uses a CNN model that records video frames of a driver's eyes. Additionally, it recognizes eye facial features using the Haar cascade classifier. An alarm system is part of the system to provide timely alerts. By providing a flexible and affordable way to lessen driving accidents caused by drowsiness, our work advances the field of intelligent safety systems for cars. In order to identify drivers” levels of tiredness in real time, this study used a hybrid approach. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Haar Cascade classifiers are integrated to monitor drivers in real time and accurately identify tiredness.

title: DyTact: Capturing Dynamic Contacts in Hand-Object Manipulation
authors: Xiaoyan Cong, Angela Xing, Chandradeep Pokhariya, Rao Fu, Srinath Sridhar
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Reconstructing dynamic hand-object contacts is essential for realistic manipulation in AI character animation, XR, and robotics, yet it remains challenging due to heavy occlusions, complex surface details, and limitations in existing capture techniques. In this paper, we introduce DyTact, a markerless capture method for accurately capturing dynamic contact in hand-object manipulations in a non-intrusive manner. Our approach leverages a dynamic, articulated representation based on 2D Gaussian surfels to model complex manipulations. By binding these surfels to MANO [66] meshes, DyTact harnesses the inductive bias of template models to stabilize and accelerate optimization. A refinement module addresses time-dependent high-frequency deformations, while a contact-guided adaptive sampling strategy selectively increases surfel density in contact regions to handle heavy occlusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyTact not only achieves state-of-the-art dynamic contact estimation accuracy but also significantly improves novel view synthesis quality, all while operating with fast optimization and efficient memory usage. Project Page: https://oliver-cong02.github.ioIDyTact.github.io/.

title: Event-Triggered Formation Cooperative Control of Mobile Robots
authors: Zhanghan Zhong
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: To address the issues of high communication frequency and large energy consumption in the formation control of multiple mobile robots, this paper proposes an event-triggered mechanism-based cooperative formation control method. Firstly, a kinematic model of the four-wheeled Mecanum wheel mobile robot is established to describe its omnidirectional motion characteristics. On this basis, a distributed formation controller based on event triggering is designed, which updates the control signal only when the error exceeds the threshold, thereby effectively reducing the number of communications. The Lyapunov method is used to prove the stability and formation consistency of the system. Simulation results show that the proposed method significantly reduces communication and control overhead while maintaining formation accuracy, verifying the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy.

title: An Improved CNN-LSTM-Based Method for Impedance Modulation Signal Prediction in Electronic Skin
authors: Shangkun Yang, Jiayi Yang, Tingting Yu
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: Electronic skin needs to integrate multimodal sensors to perceive multiple physical quantities, but the complex wiring of such systems leads to increased cost and larger volume. The multimodal sensor signals can be converted from analog to digital through resonant circuits, and multiple sensor signals can be modulated into impedance signals, allowing the measurement of multiple sensors using only two wires. However, existing studies on impedance-modulated signals suffer from insufficient feature extraction capability, making it difficult for models to effectively capture the coupling features between different frequencies, thereby limiting prediction accuracy and stability. To address this issue, this paper proposes an improved CNN-LSTM-based model (CNN-LSTM-MHR). The model incorporates a one-dimensional residual network (1D-ResNet) to enhance local feature transmission and stabilize the training process, while introducing a Multi-Head Attention mechanism to parallelly focus on the correlations and important feature weights in different subspaces of the signal. This design improves the model's global perception and key feature extraction capability for impedance-modulated signals. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms traditional models in the decoupling prediction of pressure and resistance, achieving higher prediction accuracy and stability. Specifically, the root mean square error (RMSE) is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0 0 8 0}$</tex>, the mean absolute error (MAE) is 0.0058, and the coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex>) reaches 0.9965. Compared with the baseline CNN-LSTM model, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> increases by about 1.6 %, while RMSE and MAE are reduced by nearly half. Even when only 50 sampling points are collected, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> remains at 0.9671, verifying the significant advantage of the proposed CNN-LSTM-MHR method in complex signal decoupling tasks.

title: High-Accuracy Path Servo Solution using Real-time Feedback Control
authors: Yingjie Qian, Qi Lu, Juncheng Lin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: This paper presents a novel sensor-based path-servo solution to enhance 6 Degrees-of-Freedom (DOF) path tracking performance. By utilizing real-time sensor feedback stream, which includes pose data of both tool tip and target path, the proposed system eliminates the need for additional markers or off-line programming of the path and significantly improves the robot's absolute path accuracy via real-time closed-loop control. This solution reduces requirements for precise robot positioning and calibration accuracy. Path smoothing and transition techniques ensure smooth and stable robot motion, while a time-optimized trajectory generation approach further enhances performance. Unlike laser-based tracking solutions, which are limited to 2D paths with fixed orientations, this solution can track along three-dimensional (3D) curves with changing orientations. Experimental results demonstrate path accuracy of ±0.15mm at lOmm/s and ±O.3mm at 40mm/<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$s$</tex>, showing an 80% improvement compared to robot's nominal path accuracy via off-line programming. The proposed method is applicable to various industrial applications, such as gluing, dispensing and welding in the 3C industry and vehicle manufacturing.

title: An Adaptive MPC for Full-Actuated-Propulsion UUV to Against Flow Disturbances
authors: Dianhu Kang, Zhengyi Yang, Shuhao He, Hongxu Wei, Cong Wang, Yongsheng Deng, Jiming Chen, Gaofeng Li
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: In recent years, underwater robots have undergone significant advancements. For underwater unmanned vehicles (UUVs), maintaining the stability of the base is fundamental and of utmost important for underwater operations. Among existing methods, the Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and the Model Predictive Control (MPC) have gradually become primary control strategies to resist disturbances for underwater robots. However, these methods are unable to effectively address the complex disturbances caused by varying damping coefficients in diverse underwater environments. To address this issue, we propose a method that combines an adaptive estimator with MPC, referred to as Adaptive Model Predictive Control (AMPC). The hydrodynamic damping is estimated using the pose drift data of the UUV and the flow velocities, enabling a motion control framework for underwater robots that accounts for varying hydrodynamic and flow velocities. Our method is validated on a UUV with full actuated propulsion. Simulations was conducted to evaluate the capability of the AMPC in maintaining both stationary and dynamic states. Results showed average position errors of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 8 9 m m}, \mathbf{1. 2 4 m m}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 3 1 6 m m}$</tex> in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{y}$</tex>, and z-axes, respectively, and with attitude angle errors of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.103^{\circ}, 0.374^{\circ}$</tex>, and 0.0651° in roll, pitch, yaw, respectively. These results validated the effectiveness of our proposed AMPC in controlling a UUV with fully actuated propulsion, demonstrating its resilience against external flow disturbances.

title: Modeling and Optimization of the Signal Chain in A Benchtop MPI System
authors: Jianlan Guo, Mingdong Lan, Zhiyuan Hou, Yilin Yang, Zhongyi Wu, Weixuan Feng, Haipeng Lv
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: Conventional Magnetic Particle Imaging (MPI) typically employs a drive frequency of approximately 25 kHz, which increases eddy-current heating, restricts the allowable drive amplitude, and—through more pronounced magnetic relaxation and phase lag—reduces harmonic SNR and thereby degrades spatial resolution. In contrast, lower drive frequencies reduce eddy-current heating and mitigate relaxation-related blurring, enabling larger drive amplitudes and improved safety. Motivated by this, we present a low-frequency (5.9 kHz) MPI system incorporating a pair of Halbach magnets to provide a static selection field. The excitation chain comprises a signal generator, a power amplifier, an output impedance-matching network, a 5.9 kHz band-pass filter, a single-point matching network at the coil end, and an excitation coil; multiple high-value capacitors connected in parallel reduce the equivalent series resistance and tune the coil to resonance at 5.9 kHz, while the matching network and band-pass filter efficiently deliver the target excitation and suppress third- and higher-order harmonics and out-of-band noise. The receive chain consists of a receiving coil, a high-pass filter, a low-noise amplifier, and an oscilloscope; the high-pass filter removes the fundamental, after which the amplifier boosts weak nanoparticle-generated harmonics with minimal distortion. Experiments at a 5.9 kHz drive frequency demonstrate reliable detection of higher-order harmonics, and image reconstruction from these harmonics confirms the feasibility of low-frequency MPI with improved spatial resolution and overall accuracy.

title: Incorporation of Third Harmonic Injection in Sinusoidal PWM for Enhanced Inverter Performance
authors: Subinay Sarkar, Susanta Ray
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th Conference on Applied Signal Processing (ASPCON)
abstract: This paper investigates the incorporation of third harmonic injection into sinusoidal pulse-width modulation (SPWM) to enhance inverter output voltage utilization and reduce total harmonic distortion (THD). While conventional SPWM is limited to 78.5% of the DC link voltage, the proposed method achieves a 15% higher output voltage without increasing lower-order harmonics. Simulation results demonstrate improvements in output magnitude, THD, and efficiency. Experimental validation on a laboratory prototype further confirms the findings. Additionally, efficiency and switching loss analysis are provided. The proposed technique offers a practical balance between performance gains and implementation complexity, making it suitable for electric vehicle (EV) chargers and renewable energy systems.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Early Detection of Sleep Apnea and Insomnia
authors: G. Siva Sankar Varma, K. Manikanta, Nalla Chandana, Kavali Shireesha
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: Sleep apnea, insomnia and other sleep disorders have a major influence on public health, necessitating efficient and accurate early detection mechanisms. This study presents a Machine Learning (ML) based framework for identifying these disorders using health and lifestyle indicators. The predictive performance of several categorization models, such as AdaBoost (AB), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB), Logistic Regression (LR), and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), was assessed. According to experimental results, the XGB model outperformed with 92.55% of accuracy, 89.83% of precision, 92.49% of recall, 91.04% of F1-score and 94.71% of ROC-AUC. While LR provided a fair compromise between interpretability and accuracy, KNN and LGBM also showed competitive ROC-AUC values of 95.19% and 95.05% respectively among the other models. The results demonstrate how combining behavioral and health data with cutting-edge machine learning methods might facilitate early sleep disorder screening, facilitating prompt intervention and better patient outcomes.

title: Flexible Strain Sensor Based on CVD Graphene and its Sensing Mechanism
authors: Chunyan Jiang, Shuangjie Han, Ruikai Zhai, Ying Liu
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: Flexible sensors are crucial for robotic tactile perception, enabling robots to acquire and interpret rich physical interaction data. This significantly enhances their ability for dexterous manipulation and safe interaction in unstructured environments. Cheap price, light weight with reasonable sensitivity is the application pursuit of flexible sensors. Herein, by taking advantage of the highly stretchable nature of graphene, we propose a simple and low-cost flexible and wearable Strain sensors based on thick CVD graphene. The CVD graphene is transferred and packaged by the polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). A gauge factors up to 130 is achieved from the tensile test in a strain range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{1 0 \%}$</tex>. The sensor can efficiently track the tensile deformation and rotating angle-mimicking the robot joint motion, which exhibits great commercial application potential. More importantly, the sensing mechanism-a transport channel destruction model, was established here by combining the Landauer-Büttiker formula and destructive effect of strain to on transport channels. The model fits the experimental results well. The facile sensor and its sensing mechanism provide route towards the development of the flexible sensing technology.

title: Interpretable Decision-Making for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
authors: Mona Mirzaie, Bodo Rosenhahn
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Trustworthy AI is mandatory for the broad deployment of autonomous vehicles. Although end-to-end approaches de-rive control commands directly from raw data, interpreting these decisions remains challenging, especially in complex urban scenarios. This is mainly attributed to very deep neu-ral networks with non-linear decision boundaries, making it challenging to grasp the logic behind AI-driven decisions. This paper presents a method to enhance interpretability while optimizing control commands in autonomous driving. To address this, we propose loss functions that pro-mote the interpretability of our model by generating sparse and localized feature maps. The feature activations allow us to explain which image regions contribute to the pre-dicted control command. We conduct comprehensive ab-lation studies on the feature extraction step and validate our method on the CARLA benchmarks. We also demon-strate that our approach improves interpretability, which correlates with reducing infractions, yielding a safer, high-performance driving model. Notably, our monocular, non-ensemble model surpasses the top-performing approaches from the CARLA Leaderboard by achieving lower infraction scores and the highest route completion rate, all while ensuring interpretability. Code is available here<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>https://github.com/MandM-VisionLablDTCP.

title: Self-Supervised Representation Learning with Diffusion-Based Refinement for Image Rectification
authors: Pooja Kumari, Sukhendu Das
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Distortion correction and image rectification play a vital role in improving image quality for applications such as document analysis, medical imaging, and computational photography. Existing methods often struggle to handle diverse and simultaneous lens and geometric distortions from a single distorted image, typically relying on specific features or multiple views. To address these challenges, we propose a novel two-stage framework that integrates self-supervised geometric learning with generative refinement. The first stage employs a Vision Transformer trained in a self-supervised manner to predict the original spatial arrangement of shuffled image patches, enabling robust extraction of geometric features without labeled data. These features guide a Rectification Flow Estimator to produce a coarse geometric correction. The second stage leverages a conditional stable diffusion model, conditioned on both the coarsely rectified and original distorted images, to generate fine-grained, visually consistent rectifications. This combined approach effectively handles complex distortions, overcoming limitations of previous methods that focus on single distortion types or require extensive supervision. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art techniques, offering a generalizable solution for comprehensive image rectification.

title: Pose Estimation for Connector Insertion in Robotic Peg-in-Hole Assembly
authors: Shan Liu, Yanjiang Huang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: As a fundamental industrial process, robotic peg-in-hole assembly directly governs manufacturing quality and operational efficiency. However, conventional methods often exhibit limited accuracy and poor robustness in unstructured environments, and typically rely on time-consuming and errorprone hole-searching procedures. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a robotic assembly system based on multimodal perception, featuring a hierarchical sensing architecture designed for high-precision pose estimation of industrial connectors. A custom dataset of connector instances is constructed, and deep learning techniques are employed for reliable object detection. Two distinct pose estimation strategies are developed to accommodate different structural properties of target components: one utilizes local geometric matching, while the other introduces a fusion-based optimization framework that combines visual geometric constraints with spatial structural information. Additionally, an adaptive weighting mechanism is proposed to dynamically balance the contributions from different sensing modalities. The system is deployed on a UR5 robotic arm and evaluated through automated peg-inhole insertion experiments involving USB and HDMI sockets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a significant reduction in the root mean square error of socket pose estimation compared to traditional approaches, outperforming single-modality baselines. Furthermore, the method exhibits strong generalization across various connector types, including Type-C, highlighting its applicability and scalability in real-world industrial settings.

title: EMI Response of Microcontroller I/O Ports Under Different Input Modes
authors: Shukai Li, Bing Li
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: To investigate the response pattern of embedded microcontrollers to conducted electromagnetic interference (EMI), EMI response experiments were performed using the direct power injection (DPI) method. By injecting singlefrequency continuous signals, the microcontroller's ability to identify and respond to signals above the power frequency was tested. The results indicate that in pull-up input mode, for singlefrequency signals greater than 100 MHz, the probability of the microcontroller recognizing a low logic level can significantly exceed 50%, even reaching 100%. The responses are asymmetric between pull-up and pull-down input modes. Significant waveform distortion is observed in the pull-up input mode, whereas no such distortion is found in the pull-down input mode. These findings are significant for analyzing the EMI response of circuit systems centered around embedded microcontrollers.

title: HotspotNet: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Model for Accurate Crime Hotspot Prediction
authors: S. Nirmala Sugirtha Rajini, Tonjam Gunendra Singh, Monita Wahengbam, P. Abirami, Sangeetha S, N V P. Keerthi Vembu
publish: 2025 1st IEEE Uttar Pradesh Section Women in Engineering International Conference on Electrical Electronics and Computer Engineering (UPWIECON)
abstract: The prediction of crime hotspots represents a crucial operational need for both law enforcement agencies and urban security purposes because it determines where criminal activity will occur. The individual application of CNN (Convolutional Neural Networks) and LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) networks fails to deliver effective spatial and temporal dependency capture for such purposes. The research presents HotspotNet as a newly designed DL (Deep Learning) system that combines CNN and LSTM technologies in one single structure. The HotspotNet model operates on the Communities and Crime Unnormalized dataset after data preprocessing through Min-Max scaling which combines with Autoencoder for FS(Feature Selection) before executing data dimensionality reduction. Experimental results show HotspotNet surpasses separate CNN and LSTM systems because it provides superior Mean Squared Error (MSE) scores, accuracy, and Hit Rate@K performance. HotspotNet performs better according to measurements by detecting elevated proportions of real hotspots at each place in the prediction ranking. The hybrid recommendation system demonstrates solid potential for real-time urban planning crime forecast purposes. The main contribution of this research is the design of the HotspotNet, a new hybrid CNN- LSTM architecture, which is capable of capturing spatial and time-related relationships on the crime data effectively to predict the hotspots. The combination of Min-Max normalization, Autoencoder-based FS, and DL fusion helps the model achieve impressive results and surpasses standalone CNN and LSTM methods in terms of accuracy, MSE, and HitRateatK, which proves the potential of the model to be applied in the real field of law enforcement and urban safety. The results of this study indicate that HotspotNet had better accuracy of 95.4, 0.043 MSE and HitRate of 77.1 at K, outperforming the traditional CNN and LSTM models. Such findings affirm the effectiveness of the model in providing accurate and trustworthy prediction of crime hotspots to proactively enforce the law and urban planning.

title: Temperature Prediction Using Bidirectional LSTM With Attention Mechanism
authors: Chao Xu, Fengqin You
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Machine Learning and Natural Language Processing (MLNLP)
abstract: Accurate temperature prediction is crucial for various fields such as agricultural production, energy management, and public health. This study proposes an end-to-end temperature prediction model based on Bidirectional Long ShortTerm Memory (BiLSTM) and attention mechanisms. The model employs bidirectional LSTM layers to capture both forward and backward dependencies in time-series data, while the attention mechanism enhances the feature contribution of critical time steps, thereby improving prediction accuracy. Specifically, the model first extracts multi-scale temporal features through bidirectional LSTM, then applies an attention-based weighting mechanism to highlight significant time steps, and finally outputs predictions via a single-layer LSTM decoder and fully connected layers. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method not only provides an efficient and accurate deep learning solution for short-term temperature forecasting but also improves model interpretability through the attention mechanism, offering novel perspectives and methodologies for meteorological prediction.

title: Single-Channel Digital Monopulse Tracking with Frequency Diversity Single-Sideband Time-Modulated Arrays
authors: Deng-Yue Luo, Jin-Dong Zhang, Wen Wu
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: This paper proposes a single-channel digital monopulse tracking method using frequency diversity single-side-band time-modulated array (STMA). The use of nonuniform period (NUP) and multi-step in-phase/quadrature (I/Q) modulation strategies enables the signals received by all array elements to be transmitted and recovered through a shared radio frequency (RF). With this configuration, amplitude comparison monopulse angle estimation can be achieved. Furthermore, low sidelobe levels (SLLs) in the sigma (Σ) and delta (A) patterns can be attained by weighting the recovered equivalent baseband signals. Compared with existing digital monopulse antenna arrays, the proposed method offers reduced hardware complexity and lower power consumption. The measurement and simulation results are in good agreement, validating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Generating Emotional Motion for Humanoid Robots Based on PAD-Labeled Data and Variational Autoencoder
authors: Xuchong Zhang, Jinbang Tan, Chihang Zheng
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: In the field of human-robot interaction, it is essential for humanoid robots to exhibit rich and natural emotional motion in their body language. This paper proposes a generative method that combines motion data labeled with Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance(PAD) values and a Variational Autoencoder(VAE) to produce diverse emotional motions for humanoid robots. Based on contextual requirements and basic emotion theory, 45 emotional expression motion datasets were designed and choreographed, encompassing motions of the humanoid robot's head, lumbar, and arms. PAD values were labeled according to their mapping to discrete emotions, and a multilayer perceptron(MLP) was additionally trained. By sampling specific regions of the VAE latent space, targeted emotional motions with desired styles can be generated. This approach enhances and refines the generation of specific emotional motions for humanoid robots based on emotional dimension theory.

title: Temporal and Rotational Calibration for Event-Centric Multi-Sensor Systems
authors: Jiayao Mai, Xiuyuan Lu, Kuan Dai, Shaojie Shen, Yi Zhou
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: Event cameras generate asynchronous signals in response to pixel-level brightness changes, offering a sensing paradigm with theoretically microsecond-scale latency that can significantly enhance the performance of multi-sensor systems. Extrinsic calibration is a critical prerequisite for effective sensor fusion; however, the configuration that involves event cameras remains an understudied topic. In this paper, we propose a motion-based temporal and rotational calibration framework tailored for event-centric multi-sensor systems, eliminating the need for dedicated calibration targets. Our method uses as input the rotational motion estimates obtained from event cameras and other heterogeneous sensors, respectively. Different from conventional approaches that rely on event-to-frame conversion, our method efficiently estimates angular velocity from normalflow observations, which are derived from the spatio-temporal profile of event data. The overall calibration pipeline adopts a two-step approach: it first initializes the temporal offset and rotational extrinsics by exploiting kinematic correlations in the spirit of Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA), and then refines both temporal and rotational parameters through a joint nonlinear optimization using a continuous-time parametrization in SO(3). Extensive evaluations on both publicly available and selfcollected datasets validate that the proposed method achieves calibration accuracy comparable to target-based methods, while exhibiting superior stability over purely CCA-based methods, and highlighting its precision, robustness and flexibility. To facilitate future research, our implementation will be made opensource. Code: https://github.com/NAIL-HNU/EvMultiCalib.

title: Individual Load Monitoring using Smart Energy Meter: An IoT-based Approach
authors: Abhijeet Singh, Pushpendra Singh, Neelam Srivastava, Anurag Chauhan, Vipin Agrahari, Shweta Sonkar, Sakshi Tripathi, Shivani Gautam
publish: 2025 1st IEEE Uttar Pradesh Section Women in Engineering International Conference on Electrical Electronics and Computer Engineering (UPWIECON)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive IoT-based smart energy monitoring system designed for individual load monitoring and intelligent control in residential and small commercial applications. The proposed system integrates multiple PZEM004T sensors with microcontroller units to enable real-time monitoring of critical electrical parameters, including voltage, current, power factor, and energy consumption at the individual appliance level. The multi-tier architecture incorporates cloud connectivity through ThingSpeak for data analytics and Arduino Cloud for remote control, providing consumers with granular insights into their energy consumption patterns. The system demonstrates the capability for remote monitoring and control through both web interfaces and mobile applications, thereby promoting energy efficiency awareness and enabling sustainable consumption practices through informed decision-making.

title: AM-FMT: Assisting Metric Fast Marching Tree for Motion Planning in Complex Environments
authors: Renhao Lu, Yi Cheng, Chongkun Xia, Chenyang Cao, Lunfei Liang, Xiaojun Zhu, Houde Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: This paper presents a novel sampling-based realtime (SBRT) planner for complex environments, termed the Assisting Metric Fast Marching Tree (AM-FMT). SBRT planner allows robots to plan and execute concurrently while performing online path optimization. However, their performance is influenced by the tree expansion rate and the quality of the local path, especially in a complex environment with narrow passages and dynamic obstacles. By integrating the Assisting Metric with the Real-time Fast Marching Tree (RT-FMT), our approach enables more efficient tree expansion with fewer iterations and generates local paths that effectively avoid the local minima trap before finding the global path. In addition, we propose a reactive replanning strategy that considers the motion trends of obstacles, improving the safety performance of the planner in complex scenarios with multiple dynamic and static obstacles. Experimental results in static environments demonstrate that AM-FMT outperforms RT-FMT in both the path length and arrival time. In complex dynamic environments, AM-FMT achieves the highest success rate in finding collision-free paths compared to several prominent SBRT planners.

title: A Lightweight Ensemble Learning with Uncertainty Quantification for Robust OCT Retinal Disease Prediction
authors: Madhumithaa S, Masoodhu Banu N.M, Jehosheba Margaret M
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: Automated retinal disease classification from optical coherence tomography images plays an important role in early prediction and clinical decision-making. The increasing prevalence of retinal disease worldwide necessitates the automated diagnosis. This study proposed an Ensemble learning that combined the AlexNet and NasNetMobile models to enhance the classification accuracy and prediction reliability. This study utilized the standard Kermany dataset, which consists of three retinal disease classes and the NORMAL class. Both models were trained separately with data augmentation including rotation, shear, height, width and horizontal flip as well as class balancing, and their softmax prediction probabilities were fused by using the soft voting ensemble method. To measure the uncertainty, Monte Carlo dropout was used, which evaluated the model's confidence towards its prediction. The Ensemble model achieved 99% classification accuracy, which is more than the NasNetMobile model by 1% and the AlexNet model by 2%. The proposed model also exhibited lower uncertainty mean and threshold values than the individual model. These experimental findings illustrated that the proposed model obtained better performance and robustness than an individual model. Additionally, uncertainty estimation signified incorrect classification with high uncertainty values, facilitating better interpretability and trustworthiness. This method was computationally efficient and well-suited for real-time diagnostic support for ophthalmologists,

title: Tuning-Free Multi-Event Long Video Generation via Synchronized Coupled Sampling
authors: Subin Kim, Seoung Wug Oh, Jui-Hsien Wang, Joon-Young Lee, Jinwoo Shin
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: While recent advancements in text-to-video diffusion models enable high-quality short video generation from a single prompt, generating real-world long videos in a single pass remains challenging due to limited data and high computational costs. To address this, several works propose tuning-free approaches, i.e., extending existing models for long video generation, specifically using multiple prompts to allow for dynamic and controlled content changes. However, these methods primarily focus on ensuring smooth transitions between adjacent frames, often leading to content drift and a gradual loss of semantic coherence over longer sequences. To tackle such an issue, we propose Synchronized Coupled Sampling (SynCoS), a novel inference framework that synchronizes denoising paths across the entire video, ensuring long-range consistency across both adjacent and distant frames. Our approach combines two complementary sampling strategies: reverse and optimization-based sampling, which ensure seamless local transitions and enforce global coherence, respectively. However, directly alternating between these samplings misaligns denoising trajectories, disrupting prompt guidance and introducing unintended content changes as they operate independently. To resolve this, SynCoS synchronizes them through a grounded timestep and a fixed baseline noise, ensuring fully coupled sampling with aligned denoising paths. Extensive experiments show that SynCoS significantly improves multi-event long video generation, achieving smoother transitions and superior long-range coherence, outperforming previous approaches both quantitatively and qualitatively.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>Visualizations are available at: https://syncos2025.github.io/.

title: Towards Large Scale Geostatistical Methane Monitoring with Part-Based Object Detection
authors: Adhemar de Senneville, Xavier Bou, Jean-Louis Bonne, Nicolas Dumelie, Rafael Grompone, Thibaud Ehret, Gabriele Facciolo
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Object detection is one of the main applications of computer vision in remote sensing imagery. Despite its increasing availability, the sheer volume of remote sensing data poses a challenge when detecting rare objects across large geographic areas. Paradoxically, this common challenge is crucial to many applications, such as estimating environmental impact of certain human activities at scale. In this paper, we propose to address the problem by investigating the methane production of bio-digesters in France. We first introduce a novel dataset containing bio-digesters, with small training and validation sets, and a large test set with a high imbalance towards observations without objects since such sites are rare. We develop a part-based method that considers essential bio-digester sub-elements to boost initial detections. To this end, we apply our method to new, unseen regions to build an inventory of bio-digesters. We then compute geostatistical estimates of the quantity of methane produced that can be attributed to these infrastructures in a given area at a given time.

title: Contextual-Personalized Adaptive Cruise Control via Fine-Tuned Large Language Models
authors: Ziye Qin, Xue Yao, Chuheng Wei, Ang Ji, Guoyuan Wu, Zhanbo Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Adaptive cruise control (ACC) is a widely adopted technique within advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) to alleviate driver workload and fatigue in long-distance driving or stop-and-go traffic scenarios. However, conventional ACC systems typically fail to account for drivers' preferences or changing environmental conditions, limiting their adaptability in adjusting headway. To bridge this gap, this study introduces a novel contextual-personalized ACC (CP-ACC) framework that leverages the contextual reasoning and adaptive customization potential of large language models (LLMs). Specifically, LLMs including LLaMA-3-8B and Mistral-7B are fine-tuned with a synthetically generated dataset encompassing diverse drivers' preferences (e.g., energy efficiency, comfort) and real-time contextual information (e.g., weather, traffic conditions). CP-ACC demonstrates the ability to identify, quantify, and balance competing objectives (e.g., safety and mobility) compared to linear feedback ACC and the intelligent driver model (IDM). Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) further enhances the LLMs' ability to recognize driving objectives and generate safe, context-aware longitudinal control commands, outperforming zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Overall, the proposed CP-ACC framework presents a promising direction for delivering smart, adaptive, and personalized driving assistance tailored to varying drivers' preferences and dynamic traffic environments.

title: Terminal Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Control with Extended Kalman Filter for a XYZ Series-Type Precision Positioning Stage
authors: Peiyang Zhou, Yang Shen, Xianmin Zhang, Benliang Zhu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: In the field of precision robotic manufacturing, positioning stages driven by linear motors are subject to disturbances which poses significant challenges to robustness and control accuracy. To address this issue, a Terminal Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Control (T-STSMC) method integrated with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is proposed. Simulation results show that, the KF-TSTSMC achieves precise tracking of a 0.1 m square-wave signal with a settling time of 0.5 s, maintaining a positioning error within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>, meeting the accuracy requirements for the macro stage of the macromicro composite positioning platform. Compared to traditional control methods, the proposed approach achieves higher control accuracy, superior dynamic performance, and enhanced robustness.

title: Refining Naive Annotations with Limited Expert Guidance for Semantic Segmentation: A Case Study on Underwater Echograms
authors: Melissa Cote, Amanda Dash, Julek Chawarski, Alexandra Branzan Albu, Femina Bharatkumar Senjaliya, Andrea Niemi
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Training models for supervised semantic segmentation typically requires large quantities of pixel-level annotations that are difficult to assemble in many application domains, in particular when metadata is scarce. In the context of underwater echogam analysis for environmental monitoring, metadata scarcity is manifested by a lack of ground truth and limited domain expert resources, hindering standard data annotation processes. We propose an iterative, non-interactive annotation approach that allows us to obtain large quantities of echogram annotations using minimal expert guidance. In a two-stage process, a segmentation neural network is first purposely overfitted to a very small expertly annotated set, and is then used to iteratively refine a larger set of rough, naive annotations. Experiments on the Cape Bathurst Arctic Sea Surface Acoustics (CBASSA) dataset showcase our method's capability to generate annotations for the sea surface and subsurface entrained air bubbles that approach expert quality level (within 5.5 p.p. for the intersection over union and 3 p.p. for the F1-score), starting from simple non-expert lines obtained at a fraction of the time required by experts. They also show that our method is compatible with both convolutional- and transformer-based neural networks, and pave the way for annotating large datasets resulting from long/continuous deployments for underwater environmental monitoring, at minimal cost.

title: Scaling Open-Vocabulary Action Detection
authors: Zhen Hao Sia, Yogesh Singh Rawat
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: In this work, we focus on scaling open-vocabulary action detection. Existing approaches for action detection are predominantly limited to closed-set scenarios and rely on complex, parameter-heavy architectures. Extending these models to the open-vocabulary setting poses two key challenges: (1) the lack of large-scale datasets with many action classes for robust training, and (2) parameter-heavy adaptations to a pretrained vision-language contrastive model to convert it for detection, risking overfitting the additional non-pretrained parameters to base action classes. Firstly, we introduce an encoder-only multimodal model for video action detection, reducing the reliance on parameter-heavy additions for video action detection. Secondly, we introduce a simple weakly supervised training strategy to exploit an existing closed-set action detection dataset for pre-training. Finally, we depart from the ill-posed base-to-novel benchmark used by prior works in open-vocabulary action detection and devise a new benchmark to evaluate on existing closed-set action detection datasets with-out ever using them for training, showing novel results to serve as baselines for future work. Our code is available at https://siatheindochinese.github.io/sia_act_page/.

title: DISC-GAN: Disentangling Style and Content for Cluster-Specific Synthetic Underwater Image Generation
authors: Sneha Varur, Anirudh R Hanchinamani, Tarun S Bagewadi, Uma Mudenagudi, Chaitra D Desai, Sujata C, Padmashree Desai, Sumit Meharwade
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel framework, Disentangled Style-Content GAN (DISC-GAN), which integrates style-content disentanglement with a cluster-specific training strategy towards photorealistic underwater image synthesis. The quality of synthetic underwater images is challenged by optical distortions due to phenomena such as color attenuation and turbidity. These phenomena are represented by distinct stylistic variations across different waterbodies, such as changes in tint and haze. While generative models are well-suited to capture complex patterns, they often lack the ability to model the non-uniform stylistic conditions of diverse underwater environments. To address these challenges, we employ K-means clustering to partition a dataset into style-specific domains. We use separate encoders to get latent spaces for style and content; we further integrate these latent representations via Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN) and decode the result to produce the final synthetic image. The model is trained independently on each style cluster to preserve domain-specific characteristics. Our framework demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, obtaining a Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) of 0.9012, an average Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) of 32.5118 dB, and a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 13.3728.

title: MORFI: Mutimodal Zero-Shot Reasoning for Financial Time-Series Inference
authors: Tina Khezresmaeilzadeh, Parsa Razmara, Mohammad Erfan Sadeghi, Seyedarmin Azizi, Erfan Baghaei Potraghloo
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Stock price prediction remains a complex and high-stakes task in financial analysis, traditionally addressed using statistical models or, more recently, language models. In this work, we introduce MORFI (Mutimodal Zero-Shot Reasoning for Financial Time-Series Inference), a novel, training-free framework that leverages Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for multi-modal stock forecasting. MORFI prompts a VLM with both textual representations of historical stock prices and their corresponding line charts to predict future price values. By combining numerical and visual modalities in a zero-shot setting and using carefully designed chain-of-thought prompts, MORFI captures complementary patterns that unimodal approaches often miss. We benchmark MORFI against standard baselines, including ARIMA, XGBoost, LSTM, and SOTA text-only LLM-based prompting methods. Experimental results show that MORFI reduces the error of these baselines by up to 89.83%, demonstrating the effectiveness of multi-modal inference for stock time-series analysis and highlighting the potential of VLMs in financial forecasting tasks without requiring task-specific training.

title: SPIE: Semantic and Structural Post-Training of Image Editing Diffusion Models with AI Feedback
authors: Elior Benarous, Yilun Du, Heng Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: This paper presents SPIE: a novel approach for semantic and structural post-training of instruction-based image editing diffusion models, addressing key challenges in alignment with user prompts and consistency with input images. We introduce an online reinforcement learning frame-work that aligns the diffusion model with human preferences without relying on extensive human annotations or curating a large dataset. Our method significantly improves the alignment with instructions and realism in two ways. First, SPIE captures fine nuances in the desired edit by leveraging a visual prompt, enabling detailed control over visual edits without lengthy textual prompts. Second, it achieves precise and structurally coherent modifications in complex scenes while maintaining high fidelity in instruction-irrelevant areas. This approach simplifies users' efforts to achieve highly specific edits, requiring only 5 reference images depicting a certain concept for training. Experimental results demonstrate that SPIE can perform intricate edits in complex scenes, after just 10 training steps. Finally, we showcase the versatility of our method by applying it to robotics, where targeted image edits enhance the visual realism of simulated environments, which improves their utility as proxy for real-world settings.

title: A Multimodal Physics-Informed Neural Network Approach for Mean Radiant Temperature Modeling
authors: Pouya Shaeri, Saud AlKhaled, Ariane Middel
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Outdoor thermal comfort is a critical determinant of urban livability, particularly in hot desert climates where extreme heat poses challenges to public health, energy consumption, and urban planning. Mean Radiant Temperature <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(T_{mrt})$</tex> is a key component of environmental world modeling in evaluating outdoor thermal comfort, especially in urban environments where radiation dynamics significantly impact human thermal exposure. Traditional methods of estimating <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{mrt}$</tex> rely on field measurements and computational simulations, both of which are resource-intensive. This study introduces a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) approach that integrates shortwave and longwave radiation modeling with deep learning techniques. By leveraging a multimodal dataset that includes meteorological data, built environment characteristics, and fisheye image-derived shading information, our model enhances predictive accuracy while maintaining physical consistency. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PINN framework outperforms conventional deep learning models, with the best-performing configurations achieving an RMSE of 3.50 and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> of 0.88. This approach highlights the potential of physics-informed machine learning in bridging the gap between computational modeling and real-world applications, offering a scalable and interpretable solution for urban thermal comfort assessments.

title: Towards a Resilient Citizen-Based Smart City
authors: Tam Thanh Doan
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Social Networks Analysis, Management and Security (SNAMS)
abstract: Smart city, a social system project, is important for urban development as its goal to transform city into a modern city at the real-life technology and solve local innovation challenges. The transformation is a momentous decision, because while benefits are alluring to users; however, hiding beneath are unseen risks. To discover the dangers coming from our smart city, we ask the following question: What if smart city is harmful by design? A problem which is followed by several concerns about personal data collection and privacy violation can exist in this new form of civilization. If personal data privacy is violated, the trade-off may cost lots of money, reputation and even our lives. This paper explores that most of smart city services are relying on cloud to various levels, raising a concern on security and privacy of citizen due to the effect of cloud issues. Therefore, assessing citizen’s capabilities to engage in resilience practice and proactively improving them, is crucial to mitigate damages by data breach. We propose a Citizen Resilience Assessment (CRA) framework that aims to determine the factors that can potentially affect citizen’s data privacy and hinder them in implementing resilience practice. We design a Scale of Citizen Resilience with Vulnerability Risk indicator to evaluate and utilize the vulnerability risk levels associating to the capabilities of citizen’s resilience practice. To illustrate the practicality and usefulness of the CRA framework, we implement it in case study with Canada and discuss our result.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Detection: CNN-Based Analysis and Classification Approaches
authors: Jagarlamudi Sai Saketh, Karthika. R, Angeline. J, Raahul L S, Nitheeshh R P
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is a degenerative neurological condition that progressively impairs memory and cognitive functions, ultimately obstructing the capacity to perform daily activities. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), worldwide, it is estimated that at least 55 million people have Alzheimer’s disease. Alzheimer’s disease affects someone in the world every 3 seconds. This work uses Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for Alzheimer’s detection. There are two approaches for performing classification: classifying images and classifying images with features extracted. With Inception V3, feature extraction is carried out. Image classification is done using drill bit vision, and classification using features is performed using Machine Learning (ML) classifiers. The detection of Alzheimer using CNN achieves a maximum accuracy of 98.49%, classification using images achieves an accuracy of 97%, and features classification achieves a maximum accuracy of 96.75% using random forest. Therefore, it can be concluded that detection and image classification yield better results than classification using extracted features.

title: OpenInsGaussian: Open-Vocabulary Instance Gaussian Segmentation with Context-Aware Cross-View Fusion
authors: Tianyu Huang, Runnan Chen, Dongting Hu, Fengming Huang, Mingming Gong, Tongliang Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Understanding 3D scenes is pivotal for autonomous driving, robotics, and augmented reality. Recent semantic Gaussian Splatting approaches leverage large-scale 2D vision models to project 2D semantic features onto 3D scenes. However, they suffer from two major limitations: (1) insufficient contextual cues for individual masks during preprocessing and (2) inconsistencies and missing details when fusing multi-view features from these 2D models. In this paper, we introduce OpenInsGaussian, an Open-vocabulary Instance Gaussian segmentation framework with Context-aware Cross-view Fusion. Our method consists of two modules: Context-Aware Feature Extraction, which augments each mask with rich semantic context, and Attention-Driven Feature Aggregation, which selectively fuses multi-view features to mitigate alignment errors and incompleteness. Through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, OpenInsGaussian achieves state-of-the-art results in open-vocabulary 3D Gaussian segmentation, outperforming existing baselines by a large margin. These findings underscore the robustness and generality of our proposed approach, marking a significant step forward in 3D scene understanding and its practical deployment across diverse real-world scenarios.

title: Enhancing Vision-Language Models for Zero-Shot Video Action Recognition via Visual-Textual Refinement and Improved Interpretability
authors: Adeel Yousaf, Mubarak Shah
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Extending pre-trained image-based vision-language mod-els (VLMs), such as CLIP, to video action recognition is an important yet challenging problem. Recent zero-shot approaches have enriched the textual class represen-tations using large language models (LLMs) to generate more descriptive labels, but these methods were designed for image-based tasks and leave the visual features un-touched-failing to leverage the temporal and semantic richness of videos. We propose a unified, zero-shot frame-work that extends pre-trained image-based VLMs to video action recognition by jointly enhancing both visual and tex-tual class representations, while providing inherent inter-pretability. On the visual side, we use a video-to-text model to generate natural language summaries of the query video, capturing fine-grained spatio-temporal cues that comple-ment the visual embeddings. On the textual side, we prompt an LLM to produce action-specific descriptors-including language attributes, descriptions, and hierarchical action contexts-that enrich the class label representations and improve semantic alignment. We evaluate our framework in the zero-shot setting on four standard video action recognition benchmarks-Kinetics-400, UCF-101, HMDB-51, and Something-Something- V2-using four diverse VLM back-bones. Our method is fully compatible with pre-trained image-based VLMs, enabling them to be effectively ex-tended to video action recognition without video training, and it also improves performance when applied to VLMs already adapted to video. By design, our framework also provides transparent, human-understandable justifications for each prediction.

title: Phoneme-Level Analysis for Person-of-Interest Speech Deepfake Detection
authors: Davide Salvi, Viola Negroni, Sara Mandelli, Paolo Bestagini, Stefano Tubaro
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have made the creation of speech deepfakes widely accessible, posing serious challenges to digital trust. To counter this, various speech deepfake detection strategies have been proposed, including Person-of-Interest (POI) approaches, which focus on identifying impersonations of specific individuals by modeling and analyzing their unique vocal traits. Despite their excellent performance, the existing methods offer limited granularity and lack interpretability. In this work, we propose a POI-based speech deepfake detection method that operates at the phoneme level. Our approach decomposes reference audio into phonemes to construct a detailed speaker profile. In inference, phonemes from a test sample are individually compared against this profile, enabling fine-grained detection of synthetic artifacts. The proposed method achieves comparable accuracy to traditional approaches while offering superior robustness and interpretability, key aspects in multimedia forensics. By focusing on phoneme analysis, this work explores a novel direction for explainable, speaker-centric deepfake detection.

title: Adapting Curricula by Tracking the Gap between Validation and Training Loss
authors: Raghavendra Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Curriculum learning draws inspiration from human learning, where simpler concepts are introduced first, followed by progressively more challenging ones. A key question in curriculum learning is determining the optimal pace for introducing harder concepts - progressing too slowly may result in overfitting as the model predominantly learns from simpler concepts, while introducing harder concepts too fast may lead to loss in performance through underfitting. In this paper, we propose a novel self paced curriculum learning method that adapts the pace of curriculum to the generalization error - the gap between the training and validation loss. If this gap widens, indicating potential overfitting, harder concepts are introduced more rapidly. Conversely, if the gap remains stable or narrows, additional iterations are allocated to previously introduced concepts. We demonstrate that this self-paced strategy consistently improves generalization performance in image classification tasks across various datasets, including CIFAR, Food101, and a subset of ImageNet. Our findings highlight the novel use of generalization error to pace the curriculum and encourage further exploration in the era of large-scale datasets and models.

title: High-Speed and High-Precision Comparator for SAR ADC
authors: Qin Duan, Jingwei Zhou, Guodong Su, Xiang Wang, Jun Liu
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: This paper presents a two-stage comparator for Successive Approximation Register (SAR)-assisted Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). The first-stage pre-amplifier adopts a “turn-on-when-needed” structure to reduce power consumption while maintaining high comparison accuracy. Simulation results show that the proposed design saves at least 63% power compared to conventional structures and exhibits superior noise performance. The comparator is implemented using 1.8V I/O devices in a UMC 28nm CMOS process and achieves a comparison time of 181ps with an input voltage difference of 180μV. The presented comparator is highly compatible with asynchronous SAR logic, satisfying the requirements of a 12-bit, 50MSps high-speed, high-precision SAR ADC.

title: Battery-Free Ultra-Low Power Sensor Node with Simultaneous RF Power Harvesting and Information Transmission
authors: Mengxi Yan, Xiaopeng Yao, Lei Guo, Ke Wu
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: Wireless sensor nodes are often constrained by limited power budgets, making it challenging to achieve simultaneous data transmission and reception under low-power conditions. This paper presents a battery-less ultra-low-power sensor node capable of simultaneous RF energy harvesting and information transmission without any microcontroller. The proposed design utilizes a rectifier to harvest RF energy and power the system. An envelope detector demodulates incoming amplitude-modulated signals to extract the baseband data, which is then combined with temperature information of the proposed sensor node to control the low-power voltage-controlled oscillator output. This approach enables concurrent data reception, signal forwarding, and environmental temperature sensing and transmission. The system can operate at an input power level as low as −20 dBm, and support dual-frequency output facilitating communication with nearby nodes and long-range terminals. The proposed architecture offers a promising solution for energy-constrained wireless sensor networks requiring simultaneous sensing and communication.

title: Unified AI Framework for Real-Time Customer Churn Prediction Using Behavioral and Event-Log Anomaly Signals
authors: Jyoti Kunal Shah, Nixalkumar Patel, Darsana Usha Devi, Mahendra Babu Iragala, Ashok Kumar Kunkala, Biky Chouhan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Predicting churn age among customers is one of the most important challenges when working with a digital service platform; however, the majority of existing methods utilize mostly behavioral and transactional attributes and do not consider the influence of an anomalous event in a system on customer disengagement. Abnormal event-log patterns, including atypical access control and authentication failures, are likely to have a severe impact on user behavior and affection and frequently to churn. The paper introduces a single AI-driven real-time customer churn prediction framework combining behavioral and event-log generated anomaly signals.
