authors: Qisen Wang, Yifan Zhao, Jia Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The majority of standard diffusion models employ pixel-wise degradations while neglecting multi-scale characteristics of images. Recently, generalized diffusion models with Positive Semi-definite Degradations (PSD), such as heat dissipation and blurring, have been proposed to solve it, but suffering from problems of low generation quality due to incomplete optimization analysis and non-adaptiveness to the training process and different data distributions with hand-crafted and fixed inductive biases. In this paper, we present a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the optimization process in frequency domain for PSD-based generalized diffusion models, which implies the forward process of PSD frequency domain non-isotropic degradation implicitly acting on the inductive biases of the Variational Lower Bound non-isotropic weighting in the optimization reverse process. Based on this insight, we propose the Frequency Inductive Biases Bootstrapping Optimization (FIBBO) method, which parameterizes the forward process and learns distinct frequency degradation-generation trajectories iteratively. To tackle the problem of PSD hand-crafted and fixed inductive biases, FIBBO dynamically modifies the non-isotropic Gaussian kernel of the forward degradation process so that the inductive biases introduced can be adjusted adaptively during training. Experiments on public datasets show that FIBBO makes significant improvements in the generation quality of PSD-based generalized diffusion models. The code will be publicly available.

title: Gridless Cooperative Coevolutionary Algorithm for Real-Time DOA Estimation in Automotive Radar
authors: Meiyu Fan, Jingchao Zhang, Muheng Li, Liyan Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In automotive radar systems, super-resolution angle-of- arrival estimation is required to distinguish neighboring targets for good imaging results. In order to adapt to the rapidly changing dynamic driving scenarios, the angle of arrival estimation must meet the real-time computational requirements and can be realized based on single snapshot data. Existing gridless methods based on atomic norm minimization are suitable for super-resolved angle estimation for single snapshot signals and avoid the grid mismatch problem. But the atomic norm acts as a convex relaxation of the atomic l0 norm, and it leads to a degradation of the angle resolution. Moreover, it has high computational complexity and cannot meet the real-time requirement. In order to avoid the convex relaxation and satisfy the real-time solution requirements at the same time, we propose a single snapshot multi-objective DOA estimation model. It minimizes both the measurement error term and the atomic l0 norm, and directly deals with the conflict between angle measurement accuracy and signal sparsity to jointly achieve target detection and angle estimation. To efficiently solve this NP-hard model, we design a cooperative coevolutionary crayfish optimization algorithm. The novelty is two-fold: one is the proposal of a new cooperative co-evolutionary decomposition strategy for multiple populations to efficiently decompose the multi-objective DOA estimation model into multiple single-objective problems. The crayfish optimization algorithm is applied to each population to solve the multi-objective problem with variable dimensions based on multiple swarm cooperative co-evolutionary techniques. The other innovation is the proposal of a variable-length neighboring orthogonal crossover operator for information exchange between populations. This mechanism effectively speeds up the convergence of the algorithm. Simulation results and hardware experiments show that, compared with the spatially smooth MUSIC algorithm, the atomic-norm-based gridless algorithm and its various variants, and the variational Bayesian-based gridless algorithm, our method exhibits superior performance in terms of angle measurements and target number detection. Moreover, it can realize angle measurement within 20ms, which is the only gridless algorithm among these gridless super-resolution algorithms that can meet the real-time computation requirements of automotive radar.

title: ArgusSAR: A Multi-dimensional Perception and Robust Detection Method for Multi-scale Objects in SAR Images
authors: Jinqi Zhao, Weining Sun, Zhisheng Chen, Qiang Hu, Yuxuan Li, Hongtao Shi, Yufen Niu, Zhong Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Due to the challenges presented by the different scales of objects and the complex interference in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images, current detectors struggle to achieve satisfactory accuracy and exhibit weak generalization abilities across different datasets. This paper proposes a robust and universal object detector framework named ArgusSAR, which is suitable for both ship and aircraft object detection. The proposed framework comprises three key components: In detail, a Spatial Encoder, which employs Multi-Head Attention Blocks integrated with multi-layered Encoder Blocks, extracts robust feature representations while suppressing irrelevant interference inherent in complex SAR backgrounds. Moreover, a Fine-grained Feature Aggregation Encoder, which incorporates the multi-level pyramid architecture with content-aware upsampling operations, enabling precise integration of multi-dimensional features and enhanced extraction of object boundaries. Furthermore, a robust Multi-dimensional Awareness Attention Decoder, which incorporates scale-spatial-task self-attention mechanisms to strengthen geometric perception and improve both localization accuracy and generalization ability for ship and aircraft objects. Three publicly available SAR datasets involving ship and aircraft objects are chosen to confirm the effectiveness of ArgusSAR. Compared with the baseline and the other methods, our method has the best performance on these datasets. It demonstrates that ArgusSAR framework is robust for SAR ship and aircraft objects detection. The source code is available at https://github.com/masurq/ArgusSAR.

title: Temperature, Pressure, and Strain Metamaterial Sensor Based on High-temperature Co-fired Ceramic for Extreme Environment
authors: Yi Qiao, Rui Feng, Laifang Zheng, Beile Gao, Wei Guo, Dongyang Wu, Qiulin Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: High-temperature co-fired ceramics (HTCC) exhibit great potential for sensing and monitoring applications in extreme environments. This paper presents an integrated metamaterial sensor utilizing a HTCC substrate, capable of real-time measurement of harsh environment conditions by tracking the resonance frequencies between the antenna and the sensor. A multi-resonance design was developed, featuring three distinct resonance frequencies ranging from 7 GHz to 9 GHz, allowing for the separate monitoring of temperature, pressure, and strain. The temperature-pressure-strain (TPS) sensor was shown to operate steadily between 25-1200 °C, 0-700kPa and -400 to +400με. Utilizing metamaterial technology, the sensor offers high stability, compact dimensions, which provides a reliable solution for improving the robustness of direct environmental monitoring of industrial systems in extreme environments.

title: A Novel End-to-End Framework for Low-SNR FID Signal Denoising via Rank-Sequential Truncated Tensor Decomposition
authors: Wenjingping Zhang, Hongpeng Wang, Huan Liu, Haobin Dong, Zheng Liu, Xiangyun Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: To address the challenges of extracting weak, rapidly decaying, and noise-corrupted free induction decay (FID) signals from Overhauser magnetometers, this study introduces the rank-sequential truncated tensor decomposition (RSTD) method for effective noise suppression and high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) signal recovery in high-noise environments. The proposed approach involves acquiring multichannel FID signals using an equal-delay strategy and organizing them into a third-order tensor through data segmentation. Subsequently, the tensor undergoes a higher-order singular value decomposition combined with matrix rank decomposition, following a predefined modal decomposition sequence to enhance SNR. Comparative evaluations against conventional techniques, including singular value decomposition (SVD), principal component analysis (PCA) assisted SVD (C-PCASVD), and multilinear SVD (MLSVD), demonstrate that RSTD achieves a signal-to-noise improvement ratio (SNIR) of 40 dB across initial SNRs ranging from -20 dB to 0 dB. The method matches MLSVD in denoising efficacy while outperforming SVD and C-PCASVD by more than 10 dB in SNIR. In particular, the proposed method completes processing within 100 ms, representing more than 50% improvement in computational efficiency over MLSVD. These results confirm that the proposed method substantially improves real-time processing capabilities without compromising noise reduction performance.

title: Robust Multimodal Learning Framework for Intake Gesture Detection Using Contactless Radar and Wearable IMU Sensors
authors: Chunzhuo Wang, Hans Hallez, Bart Vanrumste
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Automated food intake gesture detection is a critical task in the domain of dietary monitoring, with the potential to objectively and continuously record individuals' eating patterns, thereby improving the quality of life (QoL) for diverse populations. Wrist-worn inertial measurement units (IMUs), have been extensively explored for this task and have demonstrated promising performance. Recently, ambient-based contactless radar sensor, has also shown feasibility for intake gesture detection. This study aims to investigate whether the complementary features of wearable and contactless sensors can be effectively leveraged through multimodal learning to further enhance detection performance. Additionally, this study addresses a key challenge in multimodal learning, the reduced robustness when handling missing modalities. To this end, we propose a robust multimodal temporal convolutional network with cross-modal attention (MM-TCN-CMA) framework, designed to efficiently integrate information from IMU and radar sensors, improve detection performance, and maintain effectiveness when modality-incomplete data are encountered during the inference phase. A dataset comprising 52 meal sessions (3,050 eating gestures and 797 drinking gestures) collected from 52 participants is developed and made publicly available for validation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed fusion framework achieves a segmental F1-score improvement of 4.3% and 5.2% over unimodal-Radar and unimodal-IMU baselines, respectively. Under missing modality conditions, the framework still yields performance gains of 1.3% and 2.4% in the missing-Radar and missing-IMU scenarios, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to explore a robust multimodal learning framework combining IMU and radar. The proposed robust radar-IMU fusion framework holds potential for broader applications in other continuous, fine-grained human activity recognition (HAR) tasks.

title: Research on Cooperative Control Strategy for Converter Systems Considering Power Margin and Operating Constraints
authors: Yibin Tao, Bingjian Fan, Zhihao Yang, Ze Li, Jun Wang, Wei Ma
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Grid and Energy Engineering (SGEE)
abstract: As the number of electric vehicles increases, their charging load puts pressure on the voltage balance of the power grid. The V2G technology, by enabling two-way interaction between electric vehicles and the power grid in terms of energy, can alleviate the load on the power grid and play the role of “peak shaving and valley filling”, thereby enhancing the reliability of power supply. To reduce the harmonic distortion of the charging and discharging system and improve the response speed, this paper designs a charging and discharging system based on V2G, and focuses on studying its topological structure and control strategy. It is proposed to adopt bidirectional AC-DC and DC-DC converters to achieve bidirectional energy flow between the power grid and the battery. Charging and discharging are respectively controlled by direct power control and finite set model predictive current control to suppress current harmonics and reduce the total harmonic distortion rate (THD). At the same time, the phase-locked loop technology is improved, and the SOGI link is introduced to enhance the frequency response and phase stability speed. Through simulation verification, the THD of the designed system was reduced to 0.33% during charging and 0.48% during discharging, meeting the grid connection standards, and the response speed was significantly improved. The phase response time of the improved SOGI-DDSRF-PLL was 0.04 seconds, and the frequency stability time was 0.083 seconds. The control effect was superior to that of the traditional strategy.

title: A Comparison of Data-Driven Shape Quantification Methods for 2D Microscopy Images
authors: Anna Foix Romero, Alexander Krull, Virginie Uhlmann
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Shape is a fundamental property of cells and nuclei that informs on their function and state, and is one of the key readouts of 2D microscopy image data. Quantifying shape in an unbiased manner however remains challenging, especially in exploratory contexts where discovering phenotypes with as little prior as possible is highly desirable. In this work, we demonstrate the practical value of data-driven shape quantification methods on three publicly available microscopy image datasets of cells and nuclei. We compare the information that can be gathered from shape representations obtained with state-of-the-art self-supervised learning models and contrast it against the performance of the widely-used baseline consisting of describing shape with a collection of summary statistics. Our results identify the recently-published ShapeEmbed model as a powerful option, prompting us to propose a simpler variant, coined ShapeEmbedLite, which reduces the complexity of the approach without sacrificing performance and even achieve gains in some metrics. Through our experiments, we demonstrate that data-driven representations obtained through self-supervised learning can facilitate the visual and quantitative exploration of shape differences in 2D microscopy image data with minimal bias, and that the variations they capture align with true signal originating from different experimental conditions or biological states. These findings outline the promising outlook of such methods to drive biology discovery and to help identify rare or unknown shape phenotypes.

title: Application of Youla Parameterization Algorithm Improved by Simulated Annealing in Active Vibration Control
authors: Li Ke, Chuangfei He, Jingjing Guo, Peng Tang
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: The existence of vibration will affect equipment performance, leading to equipment structural failure and reduced working stability. Therefore, vibration should be suppressed during operation. The active vibration control algorithm has strong adaptability and a wide control frequency band. When the passive control system cannot resist vibration, adding the active control algorithm can enhance the vibration reduction performance of the system. This paper proposes an improved Youla parameterization algorithm, which can effectively suppress single - frequency, dual-frequency and multi - frequency disturbance. This algorithm uses the simulated annealing algorithm to solve the Q parameter of the Youla parameterization algorithm. By finding the global optimal solution of the Q parameter, the system can quickly reach a good stable state. Simulation results show that the improved Youla parameterization algorithm can effectively suppress disturbance, and both the convergence speed and stable state of this algorithm are better than those of the original Youla parameterization algorithm.

title: SynBalance: Harnessing Synthetic Data in Long-tailed Recognition
authors: Zhongyu Jiang, Jiarui Cai, Chang Liu, Dongsheng An, Jonathan Wu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Real-world datasets frequently exhibit long-tailed distributions, where a small number of classes dominate while the majority are underrepresented. Traditional methods, such as data resampling, loss weighting, and uniform augmentation, provide limited solutions as they fail to address the unique requirements of both head and tail classes effectively. This paper presents a novel approach to tackling the long-tail problem by integrating strategically selected synthetic data, generated with state-of-the-art models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E, to complement the original long-tailed dataset. We examine the impact of key synthetic data properties, including quantity, diversity, recognizability, and domain gap, through extensive experiments, un-covering insights into their interrelationships. Based on these findings, we propose SynBalance, a comprehensive pipeline that includes: (1) manipulating the data synthesis process to generate synthetic data with desirable properties, (2) synthetic data composition, which selects a complementary synthetic subset tailored to the imbalanced real-world distribution, and (3) SynFusion, a customized training framework designed to integrate the original dataset with the selected synthetic data for improved performance. Extensive evaluations on three widely used benchmarks demonstrate significant performance improvements, providing valuable insights into effective dataset composition and learning strategies for long-tailed distributions.

title: Are Medical Image Generative Models Biologically Trustworthy?
authors: Suhyun Ahn, Wonjung Park, Jinah Park
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: We investigate the biological trustworthiness of deep generative models in capturing intrinsic variability in medical imaging, with a focus on neurodegenerative disease and sex dimorphism. While recent generative models for medical images have demonstrated high perceptual quality, their ability to preserve biologically meaningful variations remains underexplored. In this work, we systematically evaluate two representative generative models conditioned on sex and Alzheimer's disease cognitive states. Using real brain MRI data, we first characterize sex- and disease-related anatomical variability, then assess whether the generated synthetic images accurately reflect these patterns. Despite producing visually plausible outputs, both models exhibit substantial limitations in capturing structural differences across key brain regions, indicating low biological fidelity. Our findings highlight critical gaps in current generative approaches and underscore the need for biologically grounded evaluation metrics to ensure clinically meaningful and equitable synthetic data generation.

title: Quo Vadis Handwritten Text Generation for Handwritten Text Recognition?
authors: Vittorio Pippi, Konstantina Nikolaidou, Silvia Cascianelli, George Retsinas, Giorgos Sfikas, Rita Cucchiara, Marcus Liwicki
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: The digitization of historical manuscripts presents significant challenges for Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) systems, particularly when dealing with small, author-specific collections that diverge from the training data distributions. Handwritten Text Generation (HTG) techniques, which generate synthetic data tailored to specific handwriting styles, offer a promising solution to address these challenges. However, the effectiveness of various HTG models in enhancing HTR performance, especially in low-resource transcription settings, has not been thoroughly evaluated. In this work, we systematically compare three state-of-the-art styled HTG models (representing the generative adversarial, diffusion, and autoregressive paradigms for HTG) to assess their impact on HTR fine-tuning. We analyze how visual and linguistic characteristics of synthetic data influence fine-tuning outcomes and provide quantitative guide-lines for selecting the most effective HTG model. The results of our analysis provide insights into the current capabilities of HTG methods and highlight key areas for further improvement in their application to low-resource HTR.

title: Evolving from Unknown to Known: Retentive Angular Representation Learning for Incremental Open Set Recognition
authors: Runqing Yang, Yimin Fu, Changyuan Wu, Zhunga Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Existing open set recognition (OSR) methods are typically designed for static scenarios, where models aim to classify known classes and identify unknown ones within fixed scopes. This deviates from the expectation that the model should incrementally identify newly emerging unknown classes from continuous data streams and acquire corresponding knowledge. In such evolving scenarios, the dis-criminability of OSR decision boundaries is hard to maintain due to restricted access to former training data, causing severe inter-class confusion. To solve this problem, we propose retentive angular representation learning (RARL) for incremental open set recognition (IOSR). In RARL, unknown representations are encouraged to align around inactive prototypes within an angular space constructed under the equiangular tight frame, thereby mitigating excessive representation drift during knowledge updates. Specifically, we adopt a virtual-intrinsic interactive (VII) training strategy, which compacts known representations by enforcing clear inter-class margins through boundary-proximal virtual classes. Furthermore, a stratified rectification strategy is designed to refine decision boundaries, mitigating representation bias and feature space distortion caused by imbalances between old/new and positive/negative class samples. We conduct thorough evaluations on CIFAR100 and TinyImageNet datasets and establish a new benchmark for IOSR. Experimental results across various task setups demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: Ensemble-Based Question Classification Using Bloom's Taxonomy
authors: Jeevan Pralhad Tonde, Satish Sankaye
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: Bloom's Taxonomy provides a systematic framework for classifying the cognitive complexity of assessment questions, enabling alignment between examinations and learning outcomes. However, manual classification is slow, subjective, and unsuitable for large question repositories. Existing automated methods often require large annotated datasets, lack interpretability, or fail to provide confidence measures that educator can trust. This study presents an ensemble machine learning model for automatic Bloom's level classification that balances accuracy, transparency, and practicality. The proposed model uses interpretable linguistic and structural features, including TF-IDF word vectors, first action verb, question length, and Bloom's verb matching. Five base classifiers i.e. Gradient Boosting, Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and K-Nearest Neighbors are combined through soft voting to capture complementary strengths. The ensemble achieves 0.89 accuracy, 0.89 F1-score, 0.87 Matthews Correlation Coefficient, and 0.86 Cohen's kappa on a curated dataset of 2,000 Java programming questions labeled by experts. A web-based application has been developed to enable real-time single or bulk classification, providing confidence scores to support human review. The results demonstrate that the proposed ensemble delivers state-of-the-art performance while remaining interpretable, scalable, and deployable in authentic educational contexts.

title: Federated Active Learning for Target Domain Generalisation
authors: Razvan Caramalau, Binod Bhattarai, Danail Stoyanov
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has recently enabled deep learning models to scale over distributed client data. In this context, a new area of research has emerged, extending to various vision tasks from a domain generalisation (DG) paradigm known as Federated Domain Generalisation (FDG). Common challenges in this framework, such as data heterogeneity, obtaining domain-invariant representations, and addressing privacy concerns, have been addressed in recent years. However, we have observed that all approaches consider the entire client source data, which is often heterogeneous, corrupted, or redundant. To tackle these challenges, we propose FEDALV, the first Federated Active Learning (FAL) framework for DG. Prior to implementing our sampling strategy, we establish and solve the generalisation problem with our FDG pipeline, FEDA, leveraging the free energy alignment between each source domain and the target. Furthermore, this measure facilitates FEDALV in the active learning setting by sampling the most informative source samples only from clients aligned with the target domain. Our extensive empirical study demonstrates the superiority of our method in accuracy and efficiency compared to multiple contemporary methods. FEDALV manages to achieve the performance of the full training target accuracy while sampling as little as 5% of the source client's data.

title: Digital Precognition: Teaching Transformers to Map Cyber Threats Before Analysts Can
authors: Shane Waldrop, Erdogan Dogdu, Roya Choupani, William Mitchell
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Mapping cyber threat intelligence (CTI) reports to the MITRE ATT&CK framework is essential for threat analysis but remains tedious manual work requiring specialized expertise. Prior automated approaches face a stubborn trade-off: classification methods achieve high accuracy only by restricting coverage to small technique subsets, while broader coverage tanks precision. We sidestep this trade-off by reformulating CTI-to-ATT&CK mapping as a neural reranking task. Our two-stage pipeline combines BM25 retrieval with a fine-tuned MiniLM cross-encoder, achieving 87.67% precision at rank 1 (P@1) on 146 test queries spanning 7 threat actors—or 79.45% under strict single-label evaluation. Unlike classification approaches limited to 50–200 techniques, our retrieval formulation can score any technique in the full 600+ ATT&CK vocabulary and accommodates new techniques without retraining. The system trains in 24 minutes on a laptop CPU with 55ms inference latency, making it practical for resource-constrained security teams. We also uncover a critical data alignment issue in MITRE’s TRAM dataset—only 2.4% train-test overlap—that likely hamstrung prior research efforts.

title: Sparse Hyperbolic Convolutional Networks with Enhanced Object Localization via GradCAM Analysis
authors: Vijayavallabh Jayamanikandan, Jithamanyu Settur, Lokesh Rajulapati, Raghunathan Rengaswamy
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Hyperbolic spaces model hierarchical structures within data. Studies have demonstrated that spatial representations in the hippocampus are structured within hyperbolic spaces to optimize efficiency[26]. We explore the use of hyperbolic convolutional networks with sparsity constraints (L1 and Top-k) and analyze the significance of features in the images for classification tasks using GradCAM. We show that applying sparsity constraints to hyperbolic convolutional networks yields performance comparable to established benchmarks and results in greater interpretability. This work develops sparse hyperbolic representations, enhancing interpretability in AI systems. Link to our code

title: Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models via Retriever and Prompt Optimization
authors: Xiaochen Lai, Xiaodi Pan, Yongkang Su, Genglin Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) effectively enhances large language models (LLMs) with external knowledge for more factual and accurate outputs. However, existing RAG methods often rely on manually crafted prompts or full fine-tuning, which can impair LLM generalization and increase computational costs. To provide a more parameter-efficient and empirically grounded alternative, we adopt a two-stage fine-tuning strategy that improves RAG efficiency while helping to preserve LLM capabilities. In the first stage, the retriever is fully fine-tuned to boost retrieval precision. In the second stage, a prompt-tuning module is introduced for the generator, inserting trainable virtual tokens between retrieved documents and the query to guide knowledge utilization without altering model parameters. Using E5-Base as the retriever and LLaMA-2-7B as the generator, our method is trained on MS MARCO and evaluated on five QA benchmarks. Experiments show consistent gains in EM and F1, confirming the practical effectiveness, generalizability, and parameter efficiency of our approach. While conceptually simple, this framework empirically demonstrates the strong synergy between retriever fine-tuning and prompt-based generator adaptation, offering a scalable, plug-and-play recipe for RAG enhancement.

title: Locally Controlled Face Aging with Latent Diffusion Models
authors: Lais Isabelle Alves dos Santos, Julien Despois, Thibaut Chauffier, Sileye O. Ba, Giovanni Palma
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: We present a novel approach to face aging that addresses the limitations of current methods which treat aging as a global, homogeneous process. Existing techniques using GANs and diffusion models often condition generation on a reference image and target age, neglecting that facial regions age heterogeneously due to both intrinsic chronological factors and extrinsic elements like sun exposure. Our method leverages latent diffusion models to selectively age specific facial regions using local aging signs. This approach provides significantly finer-grained control over the generation process, enabling more realistic and personalized aging. We employ a latent diffusion refiner to seamlessly blend these locally aged regions, ensuring a globally consistent and natural-looking synthesis. Experimental results demonstrate that our method effectively achieves three key criteria for successful face aging: robust identity preservation, high-fidelity and realistic imagery, and a natural, controllable aging progression.

title: Leveraging AI for Fraud Detection in the Insurance Industry: A Customer-Centric Research Study
authors: Sonal Pathak, Suhail Javed Quraishi, Arvind Kumar Dangi
publish: 2025 14th International Conference on System Modeling &amp; Advancement in Research Trends (SMART)
abstract: Fraud is another risk that has continued to pose a very serious threat to the insurance industry, as it damages the insurer's financial base and also erodes consumer trust. Thus, this research aims at establishing the effectiveness of artificial intelligence in increasing the audit trail for more effective fraud detection while keeping clients at the core. It effectively stills the perennial conflict between fraud detection, a massive area of concern within this ever-learning insurance environment, and clients' impressions. This study uses quantitative data from three insurance companies' claims data collected through machine learning methods, as well as qualitative data from 50 insurance company employees and 200 customers. Data shows that our proposed AI-based model is better than the existing rule-based model in terms of detecting more frauds, reducing false positives by 28 percent and increasing detection rates by 37 percent. Finally, the explainable AI parts of the system enable reasonable actions based on the algorithm and allay customers' concerns about bias. From the overall findings made previously, it can be concluded that customers are willing to accept the use of artificial intelligence in detecting fraud when the procedure is well explained to them or when they can appeal the decision made by the artificial intelligence. Therefore, this research provides a guide to customer-orientated AI use in fraud detection, and such information will be useful for insurance firms willing to adopt cutting-edge technologies in a socially responsible manner.

title: Design and Analysis of a Compliant Underactuated Gripper
authors: Jiayi Ou, Qinghua Zhang, Weilin Chen, Lingbo Xie, Qinghua Lu, Junjun Wu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: To address the demands of high-speed, highprecision compliant assembly tasks, this paper proposes an underactuated compliant gripper with multi-mode grasping capabilities. The gripper can adaptively grasp objects of various shapes and sizes, and features passive compliance. The main contributions of this paper are as follows: First, a two degree of freedom(DOF), spatially symmetric compliant gripper is designed based on a crank-slider mechanism, a five-bar linkage, and compliant hinges. Second, the kinematic model of the gripper is established based on the principle of minimum potential energy and motion constraint equations. A kinetostatic analysis of the gripper is conducted utilizing the principle of virtual work, leading to the relationship between the input force and the output grasping force under two distinct grasping modes. Third, through finite element analysis (FEA), the maximum error between the analytical results and the FEA results kinematic values of the gripper within its workspace was found to be 7.23%, thereby validating the accuracy of the established kinematic model. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the stable grasping force for six different sizes of shaft components, and the grasping of a 32mm shaft under varying forces was also investigated. The average errors between the analytical results and the FEA results were determined to be 3.93% and 3.95%, respectively, while the average errors between the analytical results and experimental results were 1.78% and 3.22%, respectively. These results collectively confirm the accuracy of the proposed kinetostatic model. Finally, a series of grasping experiments with multi-specification objects were conducted to further validate the grasping capabilities of each working mode. This research provides a theoretical foundation for the subsequent compliant assembly of shaft-like components.

title: WP-CLIP: Leveraging CLIP to Predict Wölfflin's Principles in Visual Art
authors: Abhijay Ghildyal, Li-Yun Wang, Feng Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Wölfflin's five principles offer a structured approach to analyzing stylistic variations for formal analysis. However, no existing metric effectively predicts all five principles in visual art. Computationally evaluating the visual aspects of a painting requires a metric that can interpret key elements such as color, composition, and thematic choices. Recent advancements in vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated their ability to evaluate abstract image attributes, making them promising candidates for this task. In this work, we investigate whether CLIP, pre-trained on large-scale data, can understand and predict Wölfflin's principles. Our findings indicate that it does not inherently capture such nuanced stylistic elements. To address this, we fine-tune CLIP on annotated datasets of real art images to predict a score for each principle. We evaluate our model, WP-CLIP, on GAN-generated paintings and the Pandora-18K art dataset, demonstrating its ability to generalize across diverse artistic styles. Our results highlight the potential of VLMs for automated art analysis. Code: https://github.com/abhijay9/wpclip

title: Generative AI for Cel-Animation: A Survey
authors: Yunlong Tang, Junjia Guo, Pinxin Liu, Zhiyuan Wang, Hang Hua, Jia-Xing Zhong, Yunzhong Xiao, Chao Huang, Luchuan Song, Susan Liang, Yizhi Song, Liu He, Jing Bi, Mingqian Feng, Xinyang Li, Zeliang Zhang, Chenliang Xu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Traditional Celluloid (Cel) Animation production pipeline encompasses multiple essential steps, including storyboarding, layout design, keyframe animation, inbetweening, and colorization, which demand substantial manual effort, technical expertise, and significant time investment. These challenges have historically impeded the efficiency and scalability of Cel-Animation production. The rise of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI), encompassing large language models, multimodal models, and diffusion models, offers innovative solutions by automating tasks such as inbetween frame generation, colorization, and storyboard creation. This survey explores how GenAI integration is revolutionizing traditional animation workflows by lowering technical barriers, broadening accessibility for a wider range of creators through tools like AniDoc, ToonCrafter, and AniSora, and enabling artists to focus more on creative expression and artistic innovation. Despite its potential, challenges like visual consistency, stylistic coherence, and ethical considerations persist. Additionally, this paper explores future directions and advancements in AI-assisted animation.

title: Robust Deepfake Detection Against Adversarial Attacks Using Feature Fusion and FGSM Defense
authors: Amandeep Singh, A. K. Verma, Garima Singh
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: This paper proposes a robust deepfake detection framework that fuses features from ResNet50, XceptionNet, and Vision Transformers (ViT), followed by a BiLSTM to capture temporal inconsistencies. Tested using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM), the model achieves over 95% accuracy on clean videos and above 80% under adversarial attacks. By combining texture, spatial, and contextual features, the system effectively detects subtle manipulations that often bypass traditional models. The results demonstrate strong accuracy and resilience, making the approach suitable for real-world applications such as social media verification, digital forensics, and content authentication.

title: A Low-Leakage 11T SRAM Cell with Sleep Transistors for Low Leakage and Improved Stability
authors: Peddakotla Vinayaka, Rohit Lorenzo, Mukku Pavan Kumar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: In this paper, we introduce a new 11T SRAM cell built using 45-nm CMOS technology. The primary objective of this work is to enhance performance in small memory designs by addressing key issues, including high leakage power, ensuring stable read and write operations, and mitigating the risk of soft errors. When the device is in hold mode, power management is facilitated by a dedicated power management transistor located in the device’s cell. This design increases the read stability noise margin (RSNM) by 125% and the write stability noise margin (WSNM) by 175% compared to the 6T cell. It also improves RSNM by 23% and WSNM by 19% when compared to the 9T cell. Moreover, the hold stability is better than the 6T version, with a 12.5% higher hold stability noise margin (HSNM) than 6T. With respect to leakage, the 11T cell shows improvement of 60% relative to 6T, 43% relative to 9T, and 67% relative to 10T. and 46.7% lower write delay than 6T and 4% lower than 9T, while read delay is reduced by 14.3% and 27% compared to 6T and 9T. Furthermore, it reduces write power by 41.72% over 9T and 64% over 6T, while read power decreases by 27.69% and 30% compared to 9T and 6 T. This makes the proposed cell very suitable for low-power, high-stability memory applications.

title: Adaptive ML-Driven Threat Prediction and Authentication Scheme for Fog Environments
authors: Saurabh Singhal, Priyank Singhal
publish: 2025 14th International Conference on System Modeling &amp; Advancement in Research Trends (SMART)
abstract: Fog computing has been a major facilitator of low-latency and intelligent IoT ecosystems through proximity of computational resource to sources of data. Nonetheless, its distributed and heterogeneous quality brings about dire security issues like tampering of data, identity spoofing and loss of trust among the fog nodes. The paper presents a proposal of an Adaptive Machine Learning-based Threat Prediction and Authentication (AML-TPA) scheme that can be used to reinforce fog security by means of intelligent and decentralized decision-making. The designed model consists of a hybrid of an Adaptive Multi-Factor Authentication (AMFA) that is based on Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) and a Threat Prediction Engine (TPE) whose based model is a machine learning-based algorithm that detects the malicious behaviors of nodes in real time. A dynamic trust assessment mechanism allows constant updating the node credibility, depending on the outcomes of the authentication procedure and the risk potential, whereas federated learning makes sure that fog-level models are retrained adaptively and without any centralized dependency. The experimental outcomes reveal that AML-TPA has 96.7 % detection accuracy, Latency of authentication is less by 38 and energy consumption is reduced by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 - 2 0 \%}$</tex> over the conventional fog security model. The suggested scheme is lightweight cryptography, adaptive intelligence, and real-time trust management balanced, which makes it applicable to large-scale deployment of IoT-Fog-Cloud.

title: Toward Low-SWaP Cognitive Agents: Neuromorphic Intelligence and FPGA-Based Deployments of Event Neural Networks
authors: Trevor Bihl, Rajashree Majumder, Zhewei Wang, Avinash Karanth, Jundong Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Traditional artificial intelligence (AI) solutions are often high-SWaP (Size, Weight, and Power), data hungry, and lack the efficiency, resilience, and autonomy exhibited by biological intelligence. As a consequence, autonomous and cognitive agents suffer from various technical bottlenecks precluding their widespread use. Neuromorphic computing offers the potential to develop learning machines that perceive, adapt, and learn continually. In this paper, we survey the state-of-the-art in neuromorphic intelligence, including advances in spiking neural networks (SNNs), emerging brain-inspired hardware platforms, supporting software stacks, and representative application domains. Key to these advances are deployment pipelines for development, evaluation, and testing of solutions. Thus, in parallel, we further present a preliminary FPGA-based deployment pipeline for both artificial neural networks (ANNs) and SNNs. This approach not only provides a practical low-SWaP alternative for edge AI but also enables head-to-head benchmarking of neuromorphic solutions against conventional neural models on reconfigurable hardware. Together, these perspectives define a roadmap toward robust, low-power cognitive systems capable of realworld autonomy across embedded and constrained environments.

title: RCENet: Recursive Concatenation and Enhancement Network for Real-Time Super-Resolution
authors: Kihwan Yoon, Ganzorig Gankhuyag, Jinwoo Jeong
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Recent advancements in edge AI have increased demand for real-time vision models that run efficiently on edge devices. However, their architectural heterogeneity in terms of compute structure, memory bandwidth, and supported tasks requires a vision model optimized for each edge device. Therefore, we present the Recursive Concatenation and Enhancement Network (RCENet), a lightweight and efficient Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) model optimized for Google Tensor Processing Units (TPUs). To optimize the architecture for Google TPUs, we first conduct a detailed analysis of computational characteristics and runtime behavior to inform the network design. As a result, RCENet leverages hardware-efficient operators and quantization-friendly modules. We further propose Operator-Selective Quantization (OSQ) combined with Quantization-Aware Distillation (QAD), tailored to the TPU architecture, to enable deployment on integer-only inference engines without compromising perceptual quality. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show that RCENet delivers competitive visual quality with significantly reduced latency and power consumption. In particular, RCENet achieves more than 70 FPS on Google TPUs, while maintaining visual quality comparable to that of much more complex models. Our method achieved second place on the Quantized Super-Resolution track of the 2025 Mo-bileAI (MAl) Challenge, demonstrating its effectiveness for real-world deployment. Our project page is available at https://rlghksdbs.github.ioIRCENet/

title: Improved A* Algorithm Based on Dynamic Heuristics and Safety Penalty
authors: Zhuo Zhang, Wei Sun, Haoqian Li, Shuai Cheng, Xiangyu Xu, Ao Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: Aiming at the defect that the classic A* algorithm in complex environments emphasizes efficiency over security, this paper proposes an improved algorithm that integrates dynamic heuristic weights and security cost constraints. By constructing an adaptive weight adjustment mechanism based on local obstacle density, it can effectively restrain excessive greedy search in dense obstacle area while ensuring the global search efficiency in open area. A security cost penalty model based on neighborhood risk quantification is introduced to constrain the high-risk path selection on the premise of avoiding misjudgment of passable narrow lanes. The two-stage post-processing strategy removes redundant nodes through the Douglas-Peucker algorithm and combines Bezier curves to optimize the path smoothness. The comparative experiments in the grid environment show that the improved algorithm significantly reduces the path risk without sacrificing the passage capacity of narrow lanes, providing an efficient and safe solution for robot navigation in complex scenarios.

title: Design of Robot Compliance Control Algorithm with Inner and Outer Loops Fusion Based on External Force Observer
authors: Zhipeng Jia, Jian Hu, Leihao Jin, Jianyong Yao, Jiawei Lin, Xiaoli Zhao, Wenxiang Deng, Zeming Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: Due to the complexity of the working environment and task requirements, industrial robots need to come into contact with the external environment. This paper proposes an improved internal feedback super-twisting algorithm and constructs a nonlinear external torque observer with generalized momentum deviation. The results show that this algorithm can effectively estimate the external force. Meanwhile, the admittance sliding mode double-loop control method was adopted. Based on the end external force obtained by the outer loop observer, a PID admittance controller was designed, which improved the dynamic response performance, achieved the convergence of the force steady-state error to zero, and greatly enhanced the force tracking accuracy. In the inner loop of the position, the non-singular integral fast terminal finite time sliding mode was designed, achieving finite-time convergence and solving the buffeting problem in sliding mode control. Finally, the proposed method was verified on the Franka Emika Panda robot.

title: Breast Cancer Detection with Topological Deep Learning
authors: Brighton Nuwagira, Adrian Rodriguez, Qiwei Li, Baris Coskunuzer
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the integration of topological data analysis (TDA) techniques with deep learning (DL) models to improve breast cancer diagnosis from ultrasound images. By leveraging persistent homology, a TDA method that captures global structural patterns, we enrich the local spatial features typically learned by DL models. We in-corporate topological features into various pre-trained architectures, including CNNs and vision transformers (VTs), aiming to enhance screening accuracy for breast cancer, which remains the most common cancer among women. Experiments on publicly available ultrasound datasets demonstrate that combining CNNs and VTs with topological features consistently yields statistically significant performance improvements. Notably, this approach also helps to address challenges faced by DL models, such as inter-pretability and reliance on large labeled datasets. Furthermore, we generalize the Alexander duality theorem to cubical persistence, showing that persistent homology remains invariant under sublevel and superlevel filtrations for image data. This advancement reduces computational costs, making TDA methods more practical for image analysis.

title: Back Home: A Computer Vision Solution to Seashell Identification for Ecological Restoration
authors: Alexander Valverde, Luis Solano, André Montoya
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Illegal souvenir collection strips an estimated five tonnes of seashells from Costa Rica's beaches each year, yet once these specimens are seized their coastal origin-Pacific or Caribbean-cannot be verified easily because the lack of information, preventing their return when are confiscated by the local authorities. To solve this issue, we introduce BackHome19K, the first large-scale image dataset annotated with coast-level labels, and propose a lightweight pipeline that infers provenance in real time on a mobile-grade cpu. A similar PaDiM-inspired anomaly filter pre-screens uploads, increasing robustness to user-generated noise. On a held-out test set the classifier attains 86.3 % balanced accuracy, while the filter rejects 93 % of 180 out-of-domain objects with zero false negatives. Deployed as a web application, the system has already processed 70 000 shells for wildlife officers in under three seconds per image, enabling confiscated specimens to be safely repatriated to their native ecosystems. The dataset is available at FIFCOIBackHome19 K.

title: A Dual Linearly Polarized Programmable Metasurface Based on PIN Diodes
authors: Houxian Zhuo, Liang Xu, Lei Zhao
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: Programmable metasurfaces with tunable electromagnetic (EM) responses are emerging as a powerful platform for manipulating EM waves. However, the demonstrated programmable metasurfaces are typically designed to control single-polarized EM waves, which limits their usage in practical applications. Here, a PIN-diode-based dual-polarization programmable metasurface (DPM) is proposed for independent manipulation of orthogonally polarized EM waves. In each meta-atom of the DPM, two diodes are integrated along the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex> directions respectively to implement tunable 180° phase modulation for the dual linearly polarized waves. To verify the design, a DPM prototype consisting of 12 × 12 meta-atoms is fabricated, and polarization-independent reflection-beam control is demonstrated experimentally. The DPM may promote the development of advanced dual-polarized metasurfaces, showing great potential in wireless communication and sensing systems.

title: ELDNet: An Eulerian-Lagrangian Decomposed Network for Solving Multiscale PDEs
authors: Ke Zhuang, Tiejun Li, Zeyu Xiong, Jianmin Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Communication (ICAIRC)
abstract: For the unsupervised solving of complex multiscale partial differential equations, this paper proposes the Eulerian-Lagrangian Decomposed Network (ELDNet). This framework decomposes the governing equations into two sub-equations describing the global principal field and local residual field: the Eulerian branch employs Physics-informed Recurrent Convolutional Neural Networks to solve the principal field, while the Lagrangian branch utilizes Fourier Neural Operators combined with a dynamic local Monte Carlo super-sampling algorithm to efficiently resolve the residual field. Experiments on the 1D Allen-Cahn equation, 2D Burgers' equation, and 2D Navier-Stokes equations demonstrate that ELDNet achieves or surpasses state-of-the-art performance in both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency compared to other unsupervised learning methods.

title: Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Algorithms for Cervical Cancer Detection with SMOTE and ANOVA Statistical Validation
authors: Rutuja Ingole, Vrushali Mahalle, Disha Moharle, Achal Bangade, Aishwarya Kadu, Bhagyashri Mankar
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: Cervical cancer is a serious public health concern, particularly in underserved communities. A practical, affordable, and scalable way to identify high-risk instances early on is through machine learning (ML). Seven supervised algorithms were compared in this study using the UCI dataset of cervical cancer risk factors: Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). Additionally, we employed SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) to address the substantial class imbalance in 36 variables observed in each of the 858 patient samples (Geisinger patients with cervical cancer tests). Additionally, we methodically imputed 3,622 missing records. The models' performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation using five relevant metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. MLP (Accuracy = 95.3%, Fl-score = 0.69) and KNN (Accuracy = 96.5%, Fl-score = 0.75, ROC-AUC = 0.89) produced the most consistent and balanced performance overall, according to the data. However, depending on the dataset's condition, the findings from Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression were inconsistent. Both Random Forest and Logistic Regression produced acceptable recall rates, but their accuracy and Fl-scores were noticeably low, suggesting that the models may have overfitted to artificial SMOTE cases. This suggests that it is necessary to confirm that the imbalance management techniques do not unreasonably skew the outcomes. The significance of the performance differences was confirmed by a one-way ANOVA test (F = 7.67, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p} < 0.0001$</tex>).

title: A Backend Optimization Module for Robust Loop Closure Detection in Multi-Agent Neural SLAM
authors: Yunzhao Chen, Junjun Wu, Qinghua Lu, Qinghua Zhang, Yingbo He, Yongqi Liu, Shiyao Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: In recent years, neural implicit scene representations have achieved significant progress in visual SLAM. However, existing implicit neural map methods primarily focus on single-agent scenarios, struggling to address loop closure detection challenges in large-scale indoor and long-sequence scenes. Multi-agent frameworks often fail to mitigate odometry drift errors due to the lack of robust loop closure detection, compromising global consistency. To tackle this, we propose a backend optimization module specifically designed for implicit neural maps, enhancing loop closure detection performance. This module introduces robust descriptors and a novel submap post-processing fusion strategy. Experimental validation across various datasets demonstrates the module's superior accuracy in loop closure detection, significantly improving closed-loop performance in multi-agent scenarios and confirming its effectiveness. This approach provides an innovative solution for loop closure optimization of implicit neural maps in distributed environments.

title: Modular Lower-Limb Exoskeleton with Soft Pneumatic Actuators and Deep Reinforcement Learning Control for Personalized Gait Rehabilitation
authors: Anuradha Hiwase, Punam Mahakalkar, Aditya Balpande, Nitin S. Thakre, Hitakshi Madankar, Vaishali Wanarkar
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: Recent advancements in rehabilitation robotics have increased accessibility to new options for mobility in the motor disabled; however, the majority of lower-limb exoskeletons are still biomechanically deficient (i.e. aligned and responsive which is not optimal for therapeutic outcomes). The present study details the development of a modular lower-limb exoskeleton that integrates soft pneumatic actuators with a reinforcement learning controller based on Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) that includes personalized gait support through utilizing muscle activity from real-time electromyographic (EMG) signals. The new exoskeleton system contrasts with traditional rigid lower-limb exoskeletons, which rely on rigid PID and Model Predictive Control, for user-adaptive, and custom motion optimization enabling continuous learning and biomechanically informed actuation. Demonstrations conducted with OpenSim simulations indicate a reduction in tracking error, decreased energy use, and increased comfort from baseline models demonstrating effectiveness and responsiveness of the system. The results herein show the promise of deep RL-driven soft exoskeletons for scalable and patient while tailored rehabilitation interventions. Hardware prototyping, in vivo validation, and clinical implementation will be key areas of future investigation across a range of patient populations.

title: CoT-Pose: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for 3D Pose Generation from Abstract Prompts
authors: Junuk Cha, Jihyeon Kim
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Recent advances in multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning have led to significant progress in image and text generation tasks. However, the field of 3D human pose generation still faces critical limitations. Most existing text-to-pose models rely heavily on detailed (low-level) prompts that explicitly describe joint configurations. In contrast, humans tend to communicate actions and intentions using abstract (high-level) language. This mismatch results in a practical challenge for deploying pose generation systems in real-world scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel framework that incorporates CoT reasoning into the pose generation process, enabling the interpretation of abstract prompts into accurate 3D human poses. We further propose a data synthesis pipeline that automatically generates triplets of abstract prompts, detailed prompts, and corresponding 3D poses for training process. Experimental results demonstrate that our reasoning-enhanced model, CoT-Pose, can effectively generate plausible and semantically aligned poses from abstract textual inputs. This work highlights the importance of high-level understanding in pose generation and opens new directions for reasoning-enhanced approach for human pose generation.

title: AI-Powered Parking Spot Recognition Based on Multimodal Large Models
authors: Kun Chen, Yu Luo, Yuhao Cao, Xin Huang
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Robotics, Intelligent Control and Artificial Intelligence (RICAI)
abstract: This paper proposes a recognition method based on the multimodal visual model GLM-4.5V for parking spot identification. Firstly, prompt engineering is employed to enhance the accuracy and relevance of the model's outputs. Based on a structured prompt formulation, the method achieves low-code implementation and improved interpretability. Subsequently, through model training and fine-tuning, a deeper integration of the model with multimodal interactions is realized. For the precise localization of parking space corners, GLM-4.5V utilizes special markers to encapsulate the coordinates of the target area, establishing an exact correspondence between linguistic descriptions and visual elements. Finally, parking space detection and parking maneuver detection are accomplished via the multimodal interactive capabilities of GLM-4.5V. Experimental results demonstrate that the overall detection speed of GLM-4.5 V surpasses that of YOLOv3 and YOLOv7, while the proposed approach offers greater comprehensiveness and convenience.

title: Low-Voltage 9T SRAM in 22 nm CMOS with Optimized Read Path for Reduced Access Time and Enhanced Stability
authors: Kartheesan K, Nakul S, Chandramauleshwar Roy
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: Noise-resilient SRAM cells are essential for modern energy-efficient VLSI systems. This paper presents a novel 9-transistor (9T) differential SRAM cell that offers improved read stability and read access time. The proposed design is based on the conventional 6T cell with the addition of a dedicated read buffer transistor and an additional pulldown path to enhance read isolation. These modifications improve the read and write stability without significantly affecting the area. Simulation results show that our proposed 9T SRAM cell achieves a balanced trade-off between performance, stability especially read, and power across a wide voltage range. The new proposed design 9T cell shows enhanced read static noise margin (RSNM) and write static noise margin (WSNM) compared to traditional 6T, LP8T and UV9T designs. At 0.8 V, the proposed cell achieves competitive read and write access times while maintaining lower write power and decent hold power. The robustness of our 9T design against threshold voltage and channel length fluctuations is explained very clearly in the sections below. The proposed 9T cell is well-suited for low-voltage SRAM applications in portable and IoT devices.

title: IRLTrees3D: A 3D Reconstruction Dataset of Trees
authors: Joseph Chai, Barry O' Sullivan, Hoang D. Nguyen
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Measuring tree dimensions is fundamentally important to effective forestry and environmental monitoring, particularly in the estimation of investable carbon stock. Three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction, therefore, has become promising for enabling high-precision measurements and AI-driven applications in this domain, but remains challenging due to environmental uncertainty and the limitations of existing methods and datasets. This paper presents a novel, high-resolution dataset of 3D vegetation models comprising trees, logs, and plants captured from diverse locations across Ireland. The reconstruction pipeline with high-quality 2D views and 3D artifacts is provided to ensure methodological transparency and reproducibility for bridging multidimensional AI and computer vision tasks. To evaluate the geometric accuracy of the reconstructed models, we employed the diameter at breast height (DBH) as a validation metric. Our evaluation yielded a mean absolute error of 0.53 cm, with a standard deviation of 0.28cm, demonstrating the reliability of our cost-effective photogrammetry approach. The release of this dataset constitutes a significant contribution to the domain of 3D reconstruction of trees, providing a robust framework for future research in the field.

title: The Future of Precision Medicine and Artificial Intelligence in Drug Discovery: A Comprehensive Analysis of Instruments and Methods
authors: Mohd Basheeruddin, Sana Qausain, Ashish Anjankar, Amir Ahmad Dar, Faez Iqbal Khan, Archana Dhok
publish: 2025 3rd DMIHER International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare, Education and Industry (IDICAIHEI)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has revolutionised drug discovery and development by improving the speed, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness of the traditional method of finding novel therapeutic candidates. Computational and meta-analytical findings are used in this review to assess AI's effectiveness at different phases of drug discovery. It highlights important AI technologies like natural language processing (NLP), machine learning (ML), and deep learning (DL), which are being used more and more in precision medicine, virtual screening, pharmacological target identification, and repurposing. A quantitative synthesis of 12 independent studies using random-effects meta-analysis (DerSimonian-Laird method) revealed a pooled accuracy of 0.57 (95% CI: 0.38-0.76) in predicting drug-target interactions (DTIs). Furthermore, the study incorporates mathematical formulations for AI model performance evaluation, including Mean Squared Error (MSE), Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) area, and precision metrics. Despite challenges such as data heterogeneity, model interpretability, and ethical governance, the integration of explainable AI (XAI), multi-omics data fusion, and real-time analytics promises to restructure the future of pharmaceutical innovation.

title: Posture Control of a Dual Hemisphere Capsule Robot*
authors: Yuhua Cao, Jinwei Zhou, Lin Li, Yongshun Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: For diagnostic and therapeutic medical applications in stomach and colon of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, we developed a dual hemisphere capsule robot (DHCR) with the passive mode for suspended posture adjustment and the active mode for rolling locomotion. Based on the coaxial effect, in which the axis of the DHCR is likely to be coincident with the universal magnetic spin vector (UMSV) orientation under the actuation of the coaxial following magnetic torque, the conversion between two modes can be navigated by the UMSV manipulation. However, during posture adjustment of the DHCR in unstructured GI surface, some indeterminate nonlinear properties such as the centroid deviation, interference and the friction coefficient are difficult to be determined. For mitigating the impacts by indeterminate factors, we propose an adaptive control method based on radial basis function (RBF) neural network, Research results show the dynamic performance of the DHCR has been improved effectively using the adaptive controller.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning for Multi-Tenant CRM Systems
authors: Nidhi Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on AI in Cybersecurity (ICAIC)
abstract: Modern CRM systems face competing demands: ultra-personalization versus stringent privacy requirements. Multi-tenant SaaS architectures containing data from multiple corporate customers on shared infrastructure create high risk of unintended data exposure and regulatory breach. This work introduces and analyzes a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for multi-tenant environments. By separating model training from data storage, a global machine learning model is refined using insights from multiple tenants without raw customer data leaving each tenant's local enclave. The synthetic dataset includes 457 instances containing complex B2B customer interactions, churn indicators, and engagement measures. Implementation uses Python with TensorFlow Federated for decentralized training and Scikit-learn for preprocessing. Results demonstrate that the federated approach achieves predictive performance comparable to centralized training while maintaining strong privacy guarantees through differential privacy mechanisms including gradient clipping and noise injection. The federated model achieves higher accuracy as compared to isolated models, demonstrating substantial collaborative advantage for resource-constrained tenants. Computational overhead is practical at 4.5 MB per communication round with convergence in 50 rounds. This work validates that high-performance predictive analytics are achievable in multi-tenant CRM without compromising confidentiality, enabling regulatory-compliant AI deployment in competitive business ecosystems.

title: Noninvasive Near-Field Sensing for Assessment of Cerebral Blood Flow Variability in Populations with Acute and Adapted High-Altitude Exposure
authors: Siqiao Liu, Cheng Zhou, Jian Sun
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: Exposure to high altitude leads to reduced barometric and oxygen partial pressures, which may impair the brain's ability to maintain stable cerebral blood flow (CBF), increasing the risk of high-altitude illnesses. To evaluate cerebrovascular dynamics under different oxygenation conditions, we developed a noninvasive CBF monitoring device based on near-field sensing and applied it for measuring CBF variability during high-altitude exposure. A total of 25 subjects were recruited and divided into three groups: ten plain populations at low altitude, five individuals with acute exposure to high altitude, and ten adapted high-altitude populations. CBF signals were recorded in the supine resting state, and the root mean square of successive differences (RMSSD) of inter-peak intervals was extracted as an index of CBF variability. Results showed that the plain population exhibited the highest RMSSD, indicating greater vascular adaptability compared with the acute and adapted high-altitude groups. The acute high-altitude group demonstrated the lowest RMSSD, reflecting reduced cerebrovascular regulation during sudden hypoxic exposure, as compensatory mechanisms had not yet been effectively activated. The adapted high-altitude population had intermediate RMSSD values, suggesting partial recovery of regulatory capacity through adaptive mechanisms, but still lower than that of the plain population. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of near-field sensing-based monitoring for noninvasive assessment of CBF variability under hypoxia and provide quantitative evidence of group-dependent differences in cerebral autoregulatory ability.

title: Design and Development of a Lightweight Vivaldi FOPEN Antenna for VHF,UHF and L-Band Defense Applications
authors: Dipanjan Dutta, A.A. Bazil Raj, T. Shanmuganantham
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Signal Processing (AISP)
abstract: This work presents the design and development of a lightweight Foliage Penetration (FOPEN) antenna operating in the Very High Frequency (VHF) and L-band spectrum, intended for applications requiring the detection of partially or fully obscured targets. The proposed antenna is optimized for directional radiation with Half-Power Beamwidths (HPBWs) of ±60° (H-plane) and ±30° (E-plane) at 0.328 GHz, ±60° (H-plane) and ±16.4° (E-plane) at 0.735 GHz, ±31.4° (H-plane) and ±23.1° (E-plane) at 1.2 GHz, and ±30° (H-plane) and ±15° (E-plane) at 1.7 GHz, ensuring focused coverage while maintaining penetration through vegetation and light cover. The lightweight aluminium–copper design, incorporating advanced structural materials and compact geometry, enables deployment on airborne and ground-based platforms while ensuring impedance matching, stable radiation patterns, linear polarization and robustness across the operational frequency range. The proposed antenna demonstrates good impedance matching at the resonant frequencies of 0.328 GHz, 0.735 GHz, 1.2 GHz, and 1.7 GHz, with respective return losses of –16.92 dB, –21 dB, –49.86 dB, and –49.71 dB. The antenna achieves bandwidths of 90 MHz (0.29–0.38 GHz), 180 MHz (0.64–0.82 GHz), and a wideband coverage of 1 GHz (1–2 GHz). The corresponding fractional bandwidths are 27.43%, 24.48%, and 83.33%, with Q-factors of 3.64, 4.08, and 1.2–1.7 across the respective bands. The measured gain values are 1.96 dB at 0.328 GHz, 4.64 dB at 0.735 GHz, 6.8 dB at 1.2 GHz, and 7.49 dB at 1.7 GHz. Furthermore, the antenna exhibits high radiation efficiency, exceeding 96.46% in the VHF band, 85.36% in the UHF band, and 78.56% within the 1–2 GHz L-band range. Simulation results confirm beam shaping, gain, and bandwidth, making the design suitable for surveillance, reconnaissance, UAVs, and ground vehicles in foliage-rich environments, with high efficiency and reliable multi-band performance

title: Modality-Specific Benchmarks and Radar Range-Doppler Envelope Classification for Multimodal Isolated Sign Language Recognition
authors: Dmitriy Sazonov, Kamrul Islam, Evie Malaia, Sevgi Z. Gurbuz
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Recognition of isolated sign language (ISL) remains a challenged as many existing methods do not adequately account for the linguistic complexity in recorded data. From this perspective, radar is a sensing modality that has gained increased interest for ISL due to its unique ability to capture the dynamic, kinematic characteristics of sign language via measurement of Doppler shift. This paper proposes a novel method, RDM-Env, for classifying radar ISL data through computation of an efficient data representation derived from the upper and lower envelopes of range-Doppler map (RDM) videos. We benchmark radar-based and video-based models, as well as decision-level fusion paradigms against the MultiMediaLIS dataset provided-for as part of the 1st ICCV Multimodal Isolated Italian Sign Language Recognition Challenge, which provides RGB video and radar RDM data. Our results show that both Uni-Sign (for video-based ISL) and RDM-Env (for radar-based ISL) surpass previous state-of-the-art models in their respective modalities for this dataset. Next, we evaluate several decision-level fusion paradigms, but observe that they degrade performance. Finally, we analyze modality-specific challenges with RDM-Env, finding that the majority of misclassifications were between fingerspelled signs.

title: Design of High-Rejection Suspended Microstrip Duplexer with Loaded Non-Resonant Nodes
authors: Yunsong Li, Yuhui Ren, Kejuan Li, Man Song, Xuhui Du, Handong Wu
publish: 2025 Cross Strait Radio Science and Wireless Technology Conference (CSRSWTC)
abstract: This paper designs a high-rejection suspended stripline duplexer based on NRN. The duplexer is decomposed into two filters for separate design. By loading non-resonant nodes(NRN), a transmission zero is introduced into the filters, which improves the out-of-band rejection capability of the filters. In addition, the reflection group delay method is used to design the common port. Simulation results show that the out-of-band rejection of the duplexer can reach 87 dB, exhibiting excellent high-rejection performance.

title: Trajectory Error Prediction of Manipulator Using Seq2Seq Gated and Adaptive Continuous-Time Recurrent Neural Network (S2S-GACTRNN)
authors: Ke Ma, Wenjie Chen, Liheng Tuo, Xingyu Hu, Haoyang Wu, Yixin Shao, Di Shi, Kunyang Wang, Chengcai Wang, Tao Zhang, Yanggang Feng, Wuxiang Zhang, Xilun Ding
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics (ROBIO)
abstract: With the widespread application of manipulators, ensuring the trajectory tracking performance during their operation has drawn increasing attention. Due to the strong fitting capabilities, neural networks can be employed to predict and generalize the trajectory errors of manipulators. In this study, we introduce a sequence-to-sequence gated and adaptive continuous-time recurrent neural network (S2S-GACTRNN) for trajectory error prediction. This approach skips the mapping relationship between joint space and end-effector trajectory errors and is directly used for the generalization and prediction of periodic trajectories in the manipulator's workspace. Exper-imental results show that the network achieves a prediction accuracy exceeding 90% for identical end-effector speeds, with an average generalization accuracy of 84% across 7 different end-effector speeds. These results demonstrate that the network effectively learns the temporal and periodic characteristics of trajectory errors.

title: Robust Anomaly Detection in Industrial Environments via Meta-Learning
authors: Muhammad Aqeel, Shakiba Sharifi, Marco Cristani, Francesco Setti
publish: 2025 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision Workshops (ICCVW)
abstract: Anomaly detection is fundamental for ensuring quality control and operational efficiency in industrial environments, yet conventional approaches face significant challenges when training data contains mislabeled samples-a common occurrence in real-world scenarios. This paper presents RAD, a robust anomaly detection framework that integrates Normalizing Flows with Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning to address the critical challenge of label noise in industrial settings. Our approach employs a bi-level optimization strategy where meta-learning enables rapid adaptation to varying noise conditions, while uncertainty quantification guides adaptive L2 regularization to maintain model stability. The framework incorporates multi-scale feature processing through pretrained feature extractors and leverages the precise likelihood estimation capabilities of Normalizing Flows for robust anomaly scoring. Comprehensive evaluation on MVTec-AD and KSDD2 datasets demonstrates superior performance, achieving I-AUROC scores of 95.4% and 94.6% respectively under clean conditions, while maintaining robust detection capabilities above 86.8% and 92.1% even when 50% of training samples are mislabeled. The results highlight RAD's exceptional resilience to noisy training conditions and its ability to detect subtle anomalies across diverse industrial scenarios, making it a practical solution for real-world anomaly detection applications where perfect data curation is challenging.

title: Research on the Product Carbon Footprint Accounting Method Based on Identification and Resolution System and Blockchain
authors: Xinli Li, Chunming Fu, Kun Wang, Peng Li, Zhenlei Liu, Xushen Zhang, Yuxi Lv, Heliang Bing
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Smart Grid and Energy Engineering (SGEE)
abstract: As the core link of energy production, transmission, and use, the carbon footprint accounting and certification of electrical equipment are of great significance for achieving low- carbon transformation in the power industry.
